questions.explanation: The Search Optimization Service is designed for selective point-lookup queries on high-cardinality columns. Clustering is better for range scans, and SOS is more cost-effective than oversizing the warehouse for lookups.

questions.question: What does the STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS parameter control?

choices.A: The duration for which a warehouse remains in a suspended state
choices.B: The maximum time a query can run before being automatically canceled
choices.C: How long results remain valid in the result cache before expiration
choices.D: The number of days that historical versions are retained for recovery

questions.explanation: STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS sets the maximum runtime for a query; if exceeded, the query is automatically canceled. This helps prevent runaway queries from consuming excessive credits.

questions.question: A query runs slowly and the Query Profile shows most time in a single full table scan with poor pruning. The filter is on a column unrelated to natural load order. What is the BEST remedy?

choices.A: Reduce the AUTO_SUSPEND interval to keep the warehouse warm
choices.B: Add a clustering key or enable Search Optimization aligned to the filtered column
choices.C: Switch to the Economy scaling policy for improved budget optimization
choices.D: Disable the query result cache to improve partition pruning

questions.explanation: Poor pruning on a filter column means micro-partitions aren't organized by that column. Clustering (for ranges) or Search Optimization (for point lookups) on that column improves pruning and reduces scanned data.

questions.question: Which file compression is applied to staged files by default when using PUT to an internal stage?

choices.A: No compression
choices.B: gzip
choices.C: bzip2
choices.D: zstd

questions.explanation: By default, PUT compresses files using gzip when uploading to an internal stage (AUTO_COMPRESS = TRUE). You can disable this or specify a different supported compression.

questions.question: Which statement about loading numeric data with COPY INTO and the TRUNCATECOLUMNS / ENFORCE_LENGTH options is correct?

choices.A: ENFORCE_LENGTH = TRUE automatically truncates strings exceeding column width
choices.B: TRUNCATECOLUMNS = TRUE truncates strings that exceed the target column length instead of erroring
choices.C: Both options apply exclusively to numeric columns in the table
choices.D: Neither TRUNCATECOLUMNS nor ENFORCE_LENGTH affects string loading

questions.explanation: TRUNCATECOLUMNS = TRUE (or equivalently ENFORCE_LENGTH = FALSE) truncates string values that exceed the target column's length rather than producing an error during load.

questions.question: Which approach is appropriate for loading a small, one-time set of values directly without staging files?

choices.A: Snowpipe
choices.B: INSERT INTO ... VALUES
choices.C: External tables
choices.D: COPY INTO from a table stage

questions.explanation: For small amounts of data, INSERT INTO ... VALUES works fine. Bulk loading via COPY INTO and Snowpipe are designed for larger file-based ingestion and are more efficient at scale.

questions.question: When using Snowpipe with cloud event notifications, what triggers the load of a new file?

choices.A: A scheduled task configured to run COPY at fixed time intervals automatically
choices.B: An event notification (e.g., S3 event) sent to Snowpipe when a file lands in the stage
choices.C: A manual COPY INTO command executed by the user each time manually
choices.D: The warehouse auto-resuming from suspension due to inactivity

questions.explanation: In auto-ingest mode, cloud storage event notifications (e.g., AWS S3 SQS/SNS, Azure Event Grid, GCP Pub/Sub) inform Snowpipe of new files, which it then loads automatically and continuously.

questions.question: Which COPY option lets you reload files that were already loaded, ignoring load history?

choices.A: FORCE = TRUE
choices.B: PURGE = TRUE
choices.C: ON_ERROR = CONTINUE
choices.D: VALIDATION_MODE = RETURN_ALL_ERRORS

questions.explanation: FORCE = TRUE instructs COPY INTO to load all files, including those already recorded in the load history, which can result in duplicate data if used carelessly.

questions.question: Which function can be used to query the load history of a specific table for the last several days?

choices.A: COPY_HISTORY (table function in INFORMATION_SCHEMA)
choices.B: QUERY_HISTORY exclusively tracks data loads and associated load operations
choices.C: The LOAD_FILES function lists files in a stage with metadata details
choices.D: STAGE_HISTORY shows all files in a stage and their last modification times

questions.explanation: The INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COPY_HISTORY table function (and the ACCOUNT_USAGE.COPY_HISTORY view) returns details about data loads into tables, including files, rows loaded, and errors.

questions.question: When unloading data, which file formats does Snowflake support for COPY INTO <location>?

choices.A: CSV and JSON only
choices.B: CSV, JSON, and Parquet
choices.C: Only Parquet
choices.D: XML and Avro only

questions.explanation: Snowflake supports unloading to delimited (CSV/TSV), JSON, and Parquet formats. (Loading supports more formats including Avro, ORC, and XML, but unloading is limited to CSV, JSON, and Parquet.)

questions.question: What is the benefit of using a storage integration over passing credentials directly in a stage definition?

choices.A: It loads data significantly faster compared to direct credential methods
choices.B: It avoids exposing secret keys, centralizes credential management, and supports IAM role-based trust
choices.C: It enables additional compression and Time Travel data recovery features
choices.D: It provides encryption of data in transit from cloud to Snowflake

questions.explanation: A storage integration delegates trust to a cloud IAM role/identity so credentials are not embedded in SQL. This is more secure and easier to manage and rotate than inline keys.

questions.question: Which option in a COPY statement maps source file columns to target table columns using a SELECT transformation?

choices.A: COPY INTO with a query in the FROM clause (transformation)
choices.B: Using PUT with a column mapping configuration in the statement
choices.C: Using MERGE to match and transform columns during insert
choices.D: Using FLATTEN to decompose and map nested semi-structured data

questions.explanation: COPY INTO supports basic transformations by selecting and reordering columns from the staged files (e.g., COPY INTO t FROM (SELECT $1, $3 FROM @stage)), enabling column reordering, casts, and omissions during load.

questions.question: Which is a valid reason to choose bulk COPY (with a user warehouse) over Snowpipe?

choices.A: You need continuous, near-real-time loading of many small files arriving
choices.B: You are doing scheduled, large-batch loads and want predictable control over compute sizing
choices.C: You want fully managed serverless ingestion with no warehouse provisioning
choices.D: You prefer per-file charges that scale with high numbers of small files

questions.explanation: Bulk COPY with a user-managed warehouse is well suited to scheduled, large-batch loads where you control warehouse size and timing. Snowpipe is better for continuous, near-real-time ingestion of incoming files.

questions.question: Which join type returns all rows from the left table and matching rows from the right, with NULLs where there is no match?

choices.A: INNER JOIN
choices.B: LEFT OUTER JOIN
choices.C: CROSS JOIN
choices.D: RIGHT OUTER JOIN

questions.explanation: A LEFT OUTER JOIN returns all rows from the left table; for rows without a match in the right table, the right columns are NULL.

questions.question: What does LATERAL FLATTEN enable in a query?

choices.A: LATERAL FLATTEN allows joining two completely unrelated tables without explicit join conditions
choices.B: Correlated flattening of arrays/objects per row, expanding nested data alongside the parent row
choices.C: LATERAL FLATTEN pivots array values into separate columns for each row
choices.D: LATERAL FLATTEN performs aggregation across all micro-partitions in parallel

questions.explanation: LATERAL FLATTEN lets you correlate the FLATTEN table function with each row of the outer query, expanding nested arrays/objects while retaining access to the parent row's columns.

questions.question: Which operation transforms rows into columns based on distinct values?

choices.A: FLATTEN
choices.B: PIVOT
choices.C: UNPIVOT
choices.D: UNION

questions.explanation: PIVOT rotates rows into columns by aggregating values for each distinct pivot value. UNPIVOT does the reverse, turning columns into rows.

questions.question: Which window function assigns a unique sequential number to rows within a partition, with no gaps, based on ordering?

choices.A: RANK()
choices.B: DENSE_RANK()
choices.C: ROW_NUMBER()
choices.D: NTILE()

questions.explanation: ROW_NUMBER() assigns a unique, gap-free sequential integer to each row within a partition per the ORDER BY. RANK and DENSE_RANK handle ties differently and can repeat values.

questions.question: What is the difference between RANK() and DENSE_RANK()?

choices.A: RANK() and DENSE_RANK() are functionally identical in all scenarios
choices.B: RANK() leaves gaps after ties, while DENSE_RANK() does not leave gaps
choices.C: DENSE_RANK() skips ranks after ties (e.g., 1,1,3) while RANK() continues sequentially
choices.D: Neither function handles ties and both treat tied values as separate ranks

questions.explanation: When ties occur, RANK() skips subsequent ranks (e.g., 1,1,3), whereas DENSE_RANK() does not skip (e.g., 1,1,2).

questions.question: Which function returns the first non-NULL value from a list of expressions?

choices.A: NVL2
choices.B: COALESCE
choices.C: IFNULL only for two args
choices.D: NULLIF

questions.explanation: COALESCE returns the first non-NULL expression among its arguments. NULLIF returns NULL if two expressions are equal; IFNULL handles only two arguments.

questions.question: What does a recursive CTE allow you to do?

choices.A: Recursive CTEs permanently cache results into materialized views
choices.B: Query hierarchical or graph data by referencing itself iteratively
choices.C: Recursive CTEs can only flatten JSON arrays and cannot traverse hierarchies
choices.D: Recursive CTEs replace window functions and should be used instead for all analytics

questions.explanation: A recursive common table expression (WITH RECURSIVE) references itself to traverse hierarchical/graph structures such as org charts or bill-of-materials, iterating until no new rows are produced.

questions.question: Which statement about Snowflake stored procedures is TRUE?

choices.A: Stored procedures cannot contain conditional logic or control flow statements
choices.B: They can contain procedural logic (loops, branching) and can perform DDL/DML
choices.C: Stored procedures can only return scalar values and cannot execute SQL statements
choices.D: Stored procedures are read-only and cannot issue INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE commands

questions.explanation: Stored procedures support procedural logic (branching, looping), can execute SQL including DDL and DML, and can perform administrative tasks. UDFs, by contrast, are limited to returning values and cannot perform DDL/DML.

questions.question: What does a caller's rights vs. owner's rights stored procedure control?

choices.A: The warehouse used determines whether a procedure runs with caller or owner privileges
choices.B: Whether the procedure runs with the privileges/context of the caller or of the procedure's owner
choices.C: The result cache behavior changes based on caller's rights vs. owner's rights settings
choices.D: The Time Travel period is determined by caller's rights vs. owner's rights configuration

questions.explanation: An owner's rights procedure executes with the owner's privileges and context (default), while a caller's rights procedure executes with the calling user's privileges, affecting which objects it can access and session context.

questions.question: Which object generates unique numbers across sessions, often used for surrogate keys?

choices.A: Stream
choices.B: Sequence
choices.C: Task
choices.D: Stage

questions.explanation: A SEQUENCE generates unique, monotonically increasing numbers that can be used for surrogate keys. Values are unique but not necessarily gap-free.

questions.question: Are values produced by a Snowflake sequence guaranteed to be contiguous (gap-free)?

choices.A: Sequence values are guaranteed to be completely contiguous with no gaps
choices.B: No, they are unique and increasing but can have gaps
choices.C: Sequences are only unique within a single database session
choices.D: Sequence values are only supported for numeric data types

questions.explanation: Sequences guarantee uniqueness and increasing order but not contiguity. Gaps can occur due to caching, rollbacks, or concurrent usage.

questions.question: Which data type would you use to store an ordered list of values in a single column?

choices.A: OBJECT
choices.B: ARRAY
choices.C: VARCHAR
choices.D: BINARY

questions.explanation: ARRAY stores an ordered, indexed collection of VARIANT values. OBJECT stores key-value pairs, and VARIANT can hold either.

questions.question: What does the TRY_CAST function do that CAST does not?

choices.A: TRY_CAST performs type conversion faster than CAST
choices.B: It returns NULL instead of raising an error when a conversion fails
choices.C: TRY_CAST only works on numeric data types and string conversions
choices.D: TRY_CAST permanently changes the underlying column data type

questions.explanation: TRY_CAST attempts the conversion and returns NULL if it fails, whereas CAST throws an error on failure. This is useful for gracefully handling messy data.

questions.question: Which clause combines the results of two queries and removes duplicate rows?

choices.A: UNION ALL
choices.B: UNION
choices.C: INTERSECT
choices.D: JOIN

questions.explanation: UNION combines two result sets and removes duplicates. UNION ALL keeps duplicates and is faster because it skips the de-duplication step.

questions.question: When creating a clone of a table, what happens to the clone's Time Travel data?

choices.A: The clone inherits the source's complete Time Travel history from its creation
choices.B: The clone starts fresh; it does not include the source's historical Time Travel data prior to the clone
choices.C: The clone shares Time Travel history bidirectionally with the source table
choices.D: Time Travel functionality is disabled on all cloned tables by default

questions.explanation: A clone references the source's current micro-partitions at clone time but does not carry forward the source's prior Time Travel history; the clone's own Time Travel begins from when it was created.

questions.question: Which function returns the current date and time in the session's time zone?

choices.A: SYSDATE()
choices.B: CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()
choices.C: GETDATE()
choices.D: TIMESTAMP()

questions.explanation: CURRENT_TIMESTAMP() returns the current timestamp in the session time zone. SYSDATE() returns UTC time regardless of session time zone.

questions.question: Which approach lets you transform staged data during loading without a separate post-load step?

choices.A: Using a SELECT transformation in the COPY INTO statement
choices.B: Using transformation flags in the GET statement
choices.C: Transforming with GET
choices.D: Post-load transformation is mandatory and must occur in a separate step

questions.explanation: COPY INTO supports a SELECT-based transformation from the stage (column reordering, casts, omissions, simple expressions), allowing some ELT-style transformation during the load itself.

questions.question: What does the IFF function do?

choices.A: Returns one of two values based on a boolean condition
choices.B: Checks if a value is NULL and converts it to a default
choices.C: Casts and converts a value to a different data type
choices.D: Flattens an array

questions.explanation: IFF(condition, true_value, false_value) is a single-line conditional that returns true_value if the condition is TRUE, otherwise false_value—similar to a simple CASE expression.

questions.question: Which is a valid restriction on standard (non-materialized) views in Snowflake?

choices.A: Standard views cannot reference other views or materialized views
choices.B: They do not store data and recompute each time they are queried
choices.C: Standard views cannot use join operations or multiple tables
choices.D: Standard views require a clustering key to be specified

questions.explanation: Standard views store no data; their defining query runs each time the view is accessed. They can include joins, reference other views, and use most SQL constructs.

questions.question: Which command would you use to change a table's column data type?

choices.A: ALTER TABLE ... MODIFY/ALTER COLUMN
choices.B: RENAME COLUMN command changes data types of existing columns
choices.C: MERGE command is used to modify column definitions
choices.D: RENAME COLUMN

questions.explanation: ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN (or MODIFY COLUMN) changes column properties such as data type (within compatible conversions), default, or nullability.

questions.question: In a data share, can the consumer modify (write to) the shared data?

choices.A: Yes, consumers have full read/write access
choices.B: No, shared data is read-only to the consumer
choices.C: Only if granted OWNERSHIP
choices.D: Only during business hours

questions.explanation: Secure Data Sharing provides read-only access to consumers. They can query and join the shared data with their own data but cannot modify the provider's underlying objects.

questions.question: Can data be shared between Snowflake accounts in different regions or cloud platforms using standard Secure Data Sharing?

choices.A: Yes, data can be shared directly without any replication using standard Secure Data Sharing
choices.B: No, standard sharing is within the same region/cloud; cross-region/cloud requires replication (or listings) first
choices.C: Yes, but only on the Azure cloud platform; cross-region sharing is not supported on AWS or GCP
choices.D: Only with reader accounts that bridge the geographic and cloud provider differences

questions.explanation: Standard Secure Data Sharing works within the same region and cloud. To share across regions or clouds, you must replicate the data to an account in the target region/cloud (or use cross-cloud auto-fulfillment for listings).

questions.question: What is a 'listing' in the context of the Snowflake Marketplace/Collaboration?

choices.A: A list of all users in an account with their roles and permission levels
choices.B: A packaged data product (a share plus metadata) that can be offered publicly or privately to consumers
choices.C: A query history log that tracks all executed statements and their performance metrics
choices.D: A warehouse configuration document that specifies compute resources and usage policies

questions.explanation: A listing wraps a share with metadata (title, description, usage terms) and can be published to the Marketplace publicly or shared privately, supporting discovery and (cross-region) auto-fulfillment.

questions.question: Which feature allows two or more parties to collaborate on combined data without exposing each other's underlying raw data?

choices.A: Reader accounts
choices.B: Snowflake Data Clean Rooms
choices.C: Network policies
choices.D: Fail-safe

questions.explanation: Data Clean Rooms enable multiple parties to run approved analyses on combined datasets while protecting each party's row-level data through controls like secure functions, row access policies, and approved query templates.

questions.question: Which command creates a share that can then have objects added and accounts granted?

choices.A: CREATE SHARE
choices.B: CREATE REPLICATION
choices.C: CREATE LISTING
choices.D: CREATE STAGE

questions.explanation: CREATE SHARE creates a share object. You then GRANT privileges on databases/schemas/objects to the share and add consumer accounts with ALTER SHARE ... ADD ACCOUNTS.

questions.question: Which privilege model applies to objects accessed through a share by the consumer?

choices.A: The consumer's roles automatically get OWNERSHIP privileges on all shared objects from the provider
choices.B: The consumer creates a database from the share and grants read privileges (IMPORTED PRIVILEGES) to its own roles
choices.C: The provider manages the consumer's roles and determines which roles have access to shared objects
choices.D: No privileges are needed; consumers automatically have full access to all shared objects

questions.explanation: A consumer creates a database from the inbound share, then grants IMPORTED PRIVILEGES / read access on that database to its own roles so users can query the shared objects.

questions.question: How does Snowflake protect data in transit?

choices.A: Data in transit is not encrypted
choices.B: All communication is encrypted using TLS (HTTPS)
choices.C: Only internal traffic is encrypted
choices.D: Encryption in transit requires Business Critical edition

questions.explanation: Snowflake encrypts all data in transit using TLS (HTTPS) by default for all editions, protecting communication between clients and Snowflake and within its services.

questions.question: What is the purpose of hierarchical key management in Snowflake's encryption?

choices.A: To allow customers to disable encryption for improved performance and query execution speed
choices.B: To use a layered key model (root, account, table, file keys) enabling automatic key rotation and re-keying
choices.C: To store encryption keys in plaintext format for rapid access and reduced computational overhead
choices.D: To share encryption keys with consumers so they can decrypt data independently

questions.explanation: Snowflake uses a hierarchy of keys (root key encrypts account keys, which encrypt table keys, which encrypt file keys). This enables automatic key rotation (every 30 days for active keys) and periodic re-keying without downtime.

questions.question: How often does Snowflake automatically rotate encryption keys that are in active use?

choices.A: Every 24 hours
choices.B: Every 30 days
choices.C: Every 90 days
choices.D: Once per year

questions.explanation: Snowflake automatically rotates active encryption keys every 30 days. Periodic re-keying of older data (annually) is also available in Enterprise edition and above.

questions.question: Which Snowflake feature provides automatic failover of databases to a secondary account for business continuity?

choices.A: Time Travel
choices.B: Replication and Failover groups
choices.C: Fail-safe
choices.D: Data Clean Rooms

questions.explanation: Replication combined with failover groups (Business Critical and above) lets you replicate databases and account objects to another account/region and fail over for disaster recovery and business continuity.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which Snowflake feature provides large language model functions (e.g., summarization, sentiment) directly in SQL?

choices.A: Snowpipe
choices.B: Snowflake Cortex
choices.C: Snowsight
choices.D: SnowCD

questions.explanation: Snowflake Cortex provides serverless AI/ML functions, including LLM functions (summarize, translate, sentiment, complete) and ML functions, callable directly via SQL or Python.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: What does Snowflake's 'serverless' compute model mean for features like Snowpipe, Tasks (serverless), and Automatic Clustering?

choices.A: They require a dedicated user-managed virtual warehouse for exclusive execution
choices.B: Snowflake provisions and manages the compute automatically, billing per-second for actual usage
choices.C: They execute on client machines and edge nodes outside of Snowflake infrastructure
choices.D: They are completely free of charge and never consume billable compute credits

questions.explanation: Serverless features use Snowflake-managed compute that scales automatically without a user warehouse. Billing is based on actual per-second resource consumption for the service.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: What is a key benefit of Snowflake's micro-partitioning being automatic?

choices.A: Users must manually define partition keys and boundaries for each table
choices.B: There is no need to manually create or manage partitions; Snowflake handles partitioning transparently
choices.C: Partitions are strictly limited to date and timestamp columns only
choices.D: Automatic micro-partitioning prevents the definition and use of clustering keys

questions.explanation: Snowflake automatically divides table data into micro-partitions as data is loaded, removing the manual partitioning burden found in traditional systems while still enabling pruning and optional clustering.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: What is 'clustering depth' a measure of?

choices.A: The total number of columns included in a clustering key definition
choices.B: The average number of overlapping micro-partitions for a given clustering key value (lower is better)
choices.C: The total physical size of the table measured in terabytes or compressed bytes
choices.D: The count of concurrent virtual warehouses that are actively accessing the table

questions.explanation: Clustering depth measures the average overlap of micro-partitions for the clustering key. A smaller depth means less overlap and better pruning; SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION reports clustering quality.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: When is defining an explicit clustering key generally recommended?

choices.A: On every small table under 100 GB to ensure optimal query performance and accelerate all queries regardless of data distribution
choices.B: On very large tables (multi-terabyte) where queries frequently filter or join on specific columns and natural clustering has degraded
choices.C: Only on temporary and session-scoped tables that are not shared with other users or applications in the account
choices.D: Never; Snowflake architecture forbids the use of explicit clustering keys and provides no mechanism for implementing them

questions.explanation: Clustering keys benefit large tables (typically TB-scale) where query patterns consistently filter/join on certain columns and the natural ordering no longer prunes well. Small tables rarely need them.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which statement about the relationship between warehouse size and query cost is accurate?

choices.A: A larger warehouse always consumes more total credits for the identical query, regardless of how queries parallelize
choices.B: A larger warehouse may finish a query faster, and because billing is per-second, total cost can be similar while latency improves
choices.C: Warehouse size has no measurable effect on query performance or latency, with all sizes performing identically
choices.D: Smaller warehouses always consume fewer total credits regardless of execution time or query parallelization patterns

questions.explanation: Because billing is per-second, a larger warehouse that runs a parallelizable query proportionally faster can cost roughly the same total credits while reducing latency. The benefit depends on how well the query parallelizes.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: What is the function of the 'XL' to 'XS' resize while a warehouse is running queries?

choices.A: Running queries are immediately moved to the new warehouse size
choices.B: Resizing affects queued and new queries; currently running queries complete on the existing clusters
choices.C: All currently executing queries are cancelled and must be resubmitted
choices.D: Resizing is not permitted while queries are actively running in the warehouse

questions.explanation: When you resize a running warehouse, queries already executing continue on the current compute; the new size applies to subsequently started (queued/new) queries.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which best describes Snowflake's approach to handling concurrency within a single-cluster warehouse?

choices.A: Unlimited queries can run concurrently without any queuing or resource contention
choices.B: Queries share the warehouse's resources; beyond a concurrency limit, additional queries queue
choices.C: Each individual query automatically receives its own dedicated compute cluster
choices.D: Concurrency levels are completely unaffected by the warehouse size configuration

questions.explanation: A single-cluster warehouse runs concurrent queries up to its resource/concurrency limits; additional queries queue. To handle higher concurrency without queuing, use a multi-cluster warehouse (Enterprise+).

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: What does MAX_CONCURRENCY_LEVEL control on a warehouse?

choices.A: The maximum warehouse size to which a single cluster can be scaled up
choices.B: The number of concurrent statements a warehouse (cluster) attempts to run before queuing or scaling out
choices.C: The Time Travel and Fail-safe data retention period in days for rolled-back changes
choices.D: The maximum number of warehouse clusters that are allowed in an account

questions.explanation: MAX_CONCURRENCY_LEVEL sets the target number of concurrent statements per cluster. Reaching it causes queries to queue (single-cluster) or trigger additional clusters (multi-cluster), and influences resource allocation per query.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which statement about Snowflake's support for both structured and semi-structured data is correct?

choices.A: Semi-structured data must be stored and managed in an entirely separate system
choices.B: Structured and semi-structured data can coexist in the same table and be queried with standard SQL
choices.C: Only structured relational data can be queried using standard SQL syntax
choices.D: Semi-structured data cannot be indexed or leveraged for partition pruning benefits

questions.explanation: Snowflake natively supports semi-structured data (via VARIANT) alongside structured columns in the same table, all queryable with SQL. It even maintains metadata enabling pruning on semi-structured paths in many cases.

questions.question: Which command shows all privileges granted to a specific role?

choices.A: SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE <role_name>
choices.B: DESCRIBE ROLE <role_name>
choices.C: LIST PRIVILEGES
choices.D: SHOW ROLES

questions.explanation: SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE <role_name> lists the privileges granted to a role. SHOW GRANTS OF ROLE shows which users/roles have been granted that role.

questions.question: What is privilege inheritance through role hierarchy?

choices.A: A child role automatically loses all inherited privileges from its parent role upon assignment
choices.B: When role A is granted to role B, role B inherits all of role A's privileges
choices.C: Users inherit privileges exclusively from the PUBLIC role, regardless of other role memberships
choices.D: Privileges cannot be inherited through role hierarchies; each role must receive direct privilege grants

questions.explanation: Roles form a hierarchy. Granting role A to role B means B inherits A's privileges. This is why SYSADMIN, when granted custom roles, can manage all the objects those roles own.

questions.question: What does the SECURITYADMIN role have that USERADMIN does not, by default?

choices.A: Ability to create and configure warehouse clusters with auto-scaling and performance policies
choices.B: The MANAGE GRANTS privilege to grant/revoke privileges globally
choices.C: Automatic ownership and control over all databases and objects created within the account
choices.D: Ability to execute queries on any object without privilege verification or access control checks

questions.explanation: SECURITYADMIN inherits USERADMIN (user/role management) and additionally has the MANAGE GRANTS privilege, allowing it to modify grants on any object across the account.

questions.question: A user belongs to multiple roles. How does the user access privileges from a role other than their default?

choices.A: All granted roles are permanently and simultaneously activated by default with all privileges combined
choices.B: They use the active role context and can switch with USE ROLE or use secondary roles
choices.C: The user must log out completely and re-authenticate to switch to a different assigned role
choices.D: Only the default role can ever be used; secondary roles cannot be activated in any configuration

questions.explanation: A session has a primary active role (set with USE ROLE) plus optional secondary roles. Privileges are evaluated against the active role(s). USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL can activate all granted roles together.

questions.question: What is the recommended way to grant a custom role access so that administrators can manage its objects?

choices.A: Grant the custom role directly to PUBLIC to ensure all users can access it through inheritance
choices.B: Grant the custom role to SYSADMIN (and ultimately into the system role hierarchy)
choices.C: Grant the custom role individually to every user in the organization for granular control
choices.D: Avoid granting the custom role anywhere and keep it isolated from all role hierarchy structures

questions.explanation: Best practice is to create custom roles and grant them to SYSADMIN (directly or via a parent custom role), so SYSADMIN can manage all objects in the account through the role hierarchy.

questions.question: Which feature can automatically detect and classify potentially sensitive columns (like PII) in your tables?

choices.A: Resource monitors, which track Snowflake credit consumption and send spending alerts
choices.B: Data Classification (with system tags / semantic categories)
choices.C: Network policies, which restrict connections to Snowflake based on approved IP address ranges
choices.D: Result cache, which stores and reuses previous query execution results for performance optimization

questions.explanation: Snowflake's Data Classification analyzes column data and assigns semantic and privacy categories (e.g., identifying email, name, SSN), which can then drive tag-based masking policies for governance.

questions.question: What is the effect of applying a network policy with only blocked IP ranges and no allowed ranges?

choices.A: Everyone attempting to connect is immediately blocked unless explicitly pre-approved in advance
choices.B: All IPs are allowed except those explicitly blocked
choices.C: Only the explicitly blocked IP ranges are permitted to access Snowflake; all others are denied
choices.D: The network policy setting has no practical effect and serves only as informational documentation

questions.explanation: If only a blocked list is specified, all addresses are allowed except the explicitly blocked ones. If an allowed list is specified, only those (minus any blocked) can connect.

questions.question: Which connectivity feature allows private, secure access to Snowflake without traversing the public internet?

choices.A: Network policies, which specify allowed and blocked IP address ranges for connection control
choices.B: AWS PrivateLink / Azure Private Link / Google Private Service Connect
choices.C: OAuth, which provides token-based authentication for external applications and client systems
choices.D: MFA, which requires additional authentication verification factors during user login

questions.explanation: Private connectivity (AWS PrivateLink, Azure Private Link, Google Private Service Connect) provides private network access to Snowflake, available with Business Critical edition and above.

questions.question: Which statement about session policies is correct?

choices.A: They manage and control customer-managed encryption keys used for at-rest data protection
choices.B: They define idle session timeout and other session behaviors
choices.C: They define warehouse resource allocation, size configurations, and auto-scaling behavior
choices.D: They apply data masking rules to sensitive columns and control value visibility per role

questions.explanation: Session policies let administrators control session parameters such as the idle session timeout, enforcing automatic session termination after inactivity for security.

questions.question: Why is key pair authentication often preferred over passwords for automated pipelines?

choices.A: It is considerably easier to memorize and type compared to storing cryptographic keys on client systems
choices.B: It avoids storing/rotating static passwords, supports non-interactive auth, and the private key can be encrypted and rotated
choices.C: It automatically disables MFA requirements for all account users, streamlining authentication
choices.D: It immediately grants the ACCOUNTADMIN role to all users who authenticate with key pairs

questions.explanation: Key pair authentication uses an RSA private/public key, enabling secure non-interactive authentication for service accounts. Keys can be encrypted with a passphrase and rotated, and Snowflake supports key rotation with two keys.

questions.question: Which view or function helps identify the most expensive queries by credit/time over a period?

choices.A: ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY
choices.B: SHOW WAREHOUSES
choices.C: INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS
choices.D: SHOW USERS

questions.explanation: The ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY view (and INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY) provides execution details—duration, bytes scanned, warehouse—useful for identifying expensive queries to optimize.

questions.question: Which scenario most justifies enabling multi-cluster auto-scaling rather than scaling up?

choices.A: A single large nightly ETL job processing complex joins and transformations
choices.B: A BI dashboard with hundreds of concurrent users running short queries at peak times
choices.C: A one-time bulk data migration between Snowflake accounts
choices.D: Reducing overall data storage and backup costs for the account

questions.explanation: High concurrency from many simultaneous users is the classic case for multi-cluster (scale out). Large, complex single queries benefit more from scaling up (a bigger warehouse).

questions.question: What is the impact of using SELECT * versus selecting only needed columns on a columnar store like Snowflake?

choices.A: No difference, since columnar storage always reads all columns regardless
choices.B: Selecting only needed columns reduces the amount of column data scanned, improving performance
choices.C: SELECT * is consistently faster than selecting specific columns
choices.D: It only affects how results are displayed and formatted in output

questions.explanation: Because Snowflake stores data columnarly, reading fewer columns means scanning less data. Selecting only required columns reduces I/O and can improve performance and cost.

questions.question: Which is the MOST cost-effective way to provide a frequently re-run, unchanged report query with sub-second response and no warehouse cost?

choices.A: Rely on the result cache (re-run identical query within 24h on unchanged data)
choices.B: Execute it on a 6XL warehouse for optimal performance consistency
choices.C: Create a new materialized view for each report execution cycle
choices.D: Disable AUTO_SUSPEND to prevent result cache loss between runs

questions.explanation: If the query is identical and underlying data hasn't changed, the result cache returns results in milliseconds without a running warehouse (no compute cost), for up to 24 hours.

questions.question: Which warehouse property, if set too high, can hide concurrency issues but increase idle cost?

choices.A: MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT set high in a multi-cluster warehouse
choices.B: AUTO_SUSPEND configured to 60 seconds for balanced cost management
choices.C: STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS parameter for query execution limits
choices.D: MAX_DATA_EXTENSION_TIME_IN_DAYS for Fail-safe retention periods

questions.explanation: Setting MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT high keeps multiple clusters always running, ensuring capacity but incurring cost even when demand is low. A lower minimum with auto-scaling is usually more cost-efficient.

questions.question: How can you reduce storage costs for large staging tables that don't require Fail-safe protection?

choices.A: Use permanent tables with 90-day Time Travel
choices.B: Use transient tables (no Fail-safe, minimal Time Travel)
choices.C: Increase the warehouse size
choices.D: Enable Search Optimization

questions.explanation: Transient tables eliminate the 7-day Fail-safe storage and can use minimal Time Travel, reducing storage costs for transient/intermediate data that can be regenerated if lost.

questions.question: What does a 'queued' state for a query in the Query Profile/monitoring usually indicate?

choices.A: The query execution encountered a fatal error during processing or due to an exception in the execution plan
choices.B: The warehouse lacked available resources/slots, so the query waited before execution
choices.C: The result was retrieved from the result cache without requiring any actual query computation or warehouse resources
choices.D: The query was manually canceled by the user before execution could begin or during the execution process

questions.explanation: Queuing means the query waited for compute resources to become available (e.g., concurrency limits reached). Remedies include a larger warehouse or multi-cluster scaling out for concurrency.

questions.question: Which statement about the Query Acceleration Service eligibility is correct?

choices.A: Query Acceleration Service accelerates all queries without selectivity, improving performance for every query in the system
choices.B: It helps queries with large scans/filters or those with outlier resource needs, identified as eligible by Snowflake
choices.C: It only works on DELETE and UPDATE data modification statements, providing no benefits for SELECT queries
choices.D: It requires the warehouse to be configured as multi-cluster with minimum cluster counts to function properly

questions.explanation: QAS benefits queries with large table scans/filtering and unpredictable spikes. Snowflake estimates eligibility; you can inspect eligible queries via SYSTEM$ESTIMATE_QUERY_ACCELERATION and ACCOUNT_USAGE.

questions.question: Which credit-saving feature should you review if a warehouse shows long periods of low utilization between bursts?

choices.A: Increasing warehouse size
choices.B: Tuning AUTO_SUSPEND and consolidating workloads
choices.C: Disabling the result cache
choices.D: Adding more clusters

questions.explanation: Low utilization with idle gaps suggests tightening AUTO_SUSPEND so the warehouse suspends during idle periods, and possibly consolidating sporadic workloads onto fewer warehouses to avoid idle costs.

questions.question: A resource monitor is set with a quota and a 'notify at 80%, suspend at 100%' policy. What happens at 80%?

choices.A: All assigned warehouses are immediately suspended
choices.B: A notification is sent but warehouses keep running
choices.C: Running queries are automatically terminated and rolled back
choices.D: The credit quota is reset to the initial amount

questions.explanation: At the 80% threshold with a notify action, the assigned recipients receive an alert but no suspension occurs. Suspension happens only when a suspend/suspend-immediately threshold is reached.

questions.question: Which clause in a file format object specifies how to interpret a field/column delimiter for CSV?

choices.A: RECORD_DELIMITER
choices.B: FIELD_DELIMITER
choices.C: SKIP_HEADER
choices.D: ESCAPE_UNENCLOSED_FIELD

questions.explanation: FIELD_DELIMITER defines the character separating fields/columns (default comma). RECORD_DELIMITER defines the row separator (default newline).

questions.question: Which file format option tells COPY to skip the first N lines, such as a header row in a CSV?

choices.A: SKIP_HEADER
choices.B: TRIM_SPACE
choices.C: NULL_IF
choices.D: ENCODING

questions.explanation: SKIP_HEADER = N skips the first N lines of each file (commonly 1 to skip a header row) during loading.

questions.question: Which option specifies which string values should be interpreted as SQL NULL during a load?

choices.A: EMPTY_FIELD_AS_NULL
choices.B: NULL_IF
choices.C: ESCAPE
choices.D: BINARY_FORMAT

questions.explanation: NULL_IF lets you list string values (e.g., 'NULL', '\\N', '') that should be converted to SQL NULL when loaded into the table.

questions.question: When loading Parquet data, what is an advantage of Parquet over CSV?

choices.A: Parquet files are consistently smaller and human-readable compared to CSV
choices.B: Parquet is columnar, typed, and compressed, enabling efficient, schema-aware loads
choices.C: Parquet cannot store nested or hierarchical data structures
choices.D: Parquet must be decompressed before Snowflake can load it

questions.explanation: Parquet is a columnar, self-describing, compressed format with typed schema and support for nested data, generally enabling more efficient and reliable loads than text-based CSV.

questions.question: Which command lists the files currently in a stage?

choices.A: SHOW FILES
choices.B: LIST @stage_name
choices.C: GET @stage_name
choices.D: DESCRIBE STAGE

questions.explanation: LIST @stage_name (or LS) lists the files staged in the specified internal or external stage, including size and last-modified metadata.

questions.question: Which is true about loading data with COPY when MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME is used for semi-structured/Parquet files?

choices.A: File columns are matched to table columns strictly by position only
choices.B: Columns in the file are matched to table columns by name, allowing schema flexibility
choices.C: It completely disables transformations in the SELECT clause
choices.D: MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME is only supported for CSV, not Parquet/JSON

questions.explanation: MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME (CASE_INSENSITIVE or CASE_SENSITIVE) maps file fields to target columns by name rather than position, useful for Parquet/JSON loads where column order may vary.

questions.question: What does the GET command do?

choices.A: Uploads files from a local directory to an internal stage
choices.B: Downloads files from an internal stage to a local directory
choices.C: Loads data directly from a stage into a table without COPY
choices.D: Lists files in a stage and returns their names and size

questions.explanation: GET downloads files from a Snowflake internal stage to a local directory on the client machine. PUT does the reverse (upload).

questions.question: Which is a recommended practice to maximize COPY load parallelism for a single large dataset?

choices.A: Provide one massive 50 GB file to maximize total throughput and parallelism across the entire warehouse
choices.B: Split the data into multiple appropriately sized files (~100–250 MB compressed) so threads can load in parallel
choices.C: Use only raw uncompressed files to eliminate decompression overhead and reduce load processing time
choices.D: Always use the smallest warehouse size (XS) to minimize load time and reduce resource consumption during bulk operations

questions.explanation: Splitting data into multiple files lets the warehouse's threads load them in parallel. A single huge file cannot be parallelized across threads, limiting throughput.

questions.question: Which Snowflake feature can automatically infer a table schema from staged Parquet/CSV files?

choices.A: INFER_SCHEMA function
choices.B: FLATTEN
choices.C: PARSE_JSON
choices.D: DESCRIBE STAGE

questions.explanation: The INFER_SCHEMA table function detects column definitions from staged files (Parquet, Avro, ORC, CSV, JSON), and can be combined with CREATE TABLE ... USING TEMPLATE to auto-create matching tables.

questions.question: What is a key consideration when using Snowpipe REST API auto-ingest vs. cloud notifications?

choices.A: Notifications require manual file listing before Snowpipe can process them or begin loading data from staged files
choices.B: REST API requires the client to call insertFiles to notify Snowpipe, while cloud notifications trigger automatically on file arrival
choices.C: REST API is exclusively for unloading data to cloud storage and cannot be used for loading incoming files
choices.D: Cloud notifications and REST API cannot be configured together on the same Snowpipe instance or stage

questions.explanation: Snowpipe can be triggered either by calling the REST API (insertFiles) from your application or by cloud event notifications that automatically inform Snowpipe when new files land in the stage.

questions.question: Which statement deletes rows from a target table that match a condition in a MERGE?

choices.A: WHEN MATCHED THEN DELETE
choices.B: WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN DELETE
choices.C: DELETE MERGE
choices.D: MERGE DELETE ALL

questions.explanation: In a MERGE, the WHEN MATCHED THEN DELETE clause removes target rows that match the join condition (and any additional predicate). WHEN MATCHED can also UPDATE.

questions.question: Which is true about INSERT OVERWRITE?

choices.A: It appends rows to the existing table without removing data
choices.B: It truncates the table and then inserts the new rows in one operation
choices.C: It updates rows matching a specific condition
choices.D: It is identical to MERGE operations

questions.explanation: INSERT OVERWRITE replaces the table's existing contents—effectively truncating then inserting the query results—within a single atomic statement.

questions.question: Which function returns the data type of a VARIANT value at runtime?

choices.A: TYPEOF
choices.B: TO_VARIANT
choices.C: IS_VARIANT
choices.D: CHECK_TYPE

questions.explanation: TYPEOF returns the data type of a VARIANT expression as a string (e.g., 'INTEGER', 'VARCHAR', 'ARRAY', 'OBJECT'), useful when inspecting semi-structured data.

questions.question: When querying a VARIANT column path like data:address.city, what is the result's data type by default?

choices.A: The result is always VARCHAR (string) when accessing VARIANT paths
choices.B: VARIANT (which may need explicit casting to a target type)
choices.C: The result is always converted to NUMBER type automatically
choices.D: The result is always BOOLEAN

questions.explanation: Accessing a path in a VARIANT returns a VARIANT value. To use it as a specific type (e.g., string), you typically cast it with ::STRING or use functions like TO_VARCHAR.

questions.question: Which aggregate function collects values from multiple rows into a single ARRAY?

choices.A: ARRAY_AGG
choices.B: LISTAGG
choices.C: OBJECT_AGG
choices.D: ARRAY_CONSTRUCT

questions.explanation: ARRAY_AGG aggregates values from a group into a single ARRAY. LISTAGG concatenates values into a delimited string, and OBJECT_AGG builds an OBJECT from key-value pairs.

questions.question: What is the difference between LISTAGG and ARRAY_AGG?

choices.A: LISTAGG and ARRAY_AGG are functionally equivalent
choices.B: LISTAGG returns a delimited string; ARRAY_AGG returns an ARRAY (VARIANT)
choices.C: ARRAY_AGG returns a delimited string and LISTAGG returns an array
choices.D: Neither LISTAGG nor ARRAY_AGG can be used in GROUP BY clauses

questions.explanation: LISTAGG concatenates grouped values into a single delimited string, while ARRAY_AGG returns the grouped values as an ARRAY that can be further processed with array functions.

questions.question: Which statement about Snowflake tasks scheduling is correct?

choices.A: Tasks can only be scheduled to run once per account
choices.B: Tasks can be scheduled using a CRON expression or a fixed interval (e.g., every N minutes)
choices.C: Tasks require an external scheduler like Apache Airflow or Cloud Scheduler
choices.D: Tasks cannot execute stored procedures or call user-defined functions

questions.explanation: Tasks support scheduling via SCHEDULE with either a time interval (e.g., '5 MINUTE') or a CRON expression including a time zone, and can run SQL, calls to procedures, or be child tasks in a DAG.

questions.question: In a task DAG, how is a child task triggered?

choices.A: A child task runs on its own independent schedule without dependency
choices.B: After its predecessor (parent) task(s) complete successfully, via the AFTER clause
choices.C: A child task is triggered only through manual execution by administrators
choices.D: A child task is triggered exclusively when a stream's offset advances

questions.explanation: Child tasks defined with AFTER <parent_task> run after their predecessor tasks complete. Only the root task has a schedule; downstream tasks form a dependency DAG.

questions.question: Which command is used to manually start a serverless or warehouse task immediately for testing?

choices.A: EXECUTE TASK <task_name>
choices.B: RUN TASK
choices.C: CALL TASK
choices.D: START TASK

questions.explanation: EXECUTE TASK manually triggers a single run of a task (and its DAG, if a root task) immediately, which is useful for testing without waiting for the schedule.

questions.question: Which type of stream tracks only INSERT operations and ignores updates/deletes, useful for append-only sources?

choices.A: A standard stream tracks all DML operations
choices.B: An insert-only stream is different from an append-only stream
choices.C: Both B and C describe stream types
choices.D: Both B and C describe the same append-only behavior

questions.explanation: An append-only stream (also called insert-only for certain sources like external tables/directory tables) records only inserted rows, ignoring updates and deletes—efficient for append-only ingestion patterns.

questions.question: Which is required before you can replicate a database to another account in a different region?

choices.A: Nothing is required; replication is automatic and occurs without any configuration or setup in the account
choices.B: The databases/accounts must be linked under the same organization and replication enabled, then create a secondary (replica) database
choices.C: Both accounts must be Standard edition or higher to support database replication and cross-region capabilities
choices.D: A reader account must exist in the target region before replication can be initiated or configured

questions.explanation: To replicate, accounts are typically part of the same organization, replication is enabled, and you create a secondary database from the primary. Cross-region/cloud replication may have additional considerations.

questions.question: What is the difference between Time Travel and Fail-safe?

choices.A: They are the same feature with identical behavior and user accessibility, differing only in the name used in the documentation
choices.B: Time Travel is user-accessible recovery within the retention period; Fail-safe is a non-configurable 7-day period recoverable only by Snowflake support
choices.C: Fail-safe is the user-accessible window for directly recovering data, while Time Travel can be reached only by Snowflake support staff
choices.D: Time Travel always lasts exactly 7 days and Fail-safe always lasts 90 days, including for transient and temporary table objects

questions.explanation: Time Travel lets users query/restore historical data within the configured retention (up to 90 days). Fail-safe is an additional, non-configurable 7-day period after Time Travel, accessible only by Snowflake for disaster recovery.

questions.question: Which objects do NOT incur Fail-safe storage costs?

choices.A: Permanent tables
choices.B: Transient and temporary tables
choices.C: All tables
choices.D: Materialized views only

questions.explanation: Transient and temporary tables have no Fail-safe, so they avoid the associated 7-day storage cost. Permanent tables include Fail-safe storage.

questions.question: If you set DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 on a permanent table, what is the effect?

choices.A: It disables Time Travel for that table (no historical querying), but Fail-safe still applies
choices.B: It disables the Fail-safe period on the table, removing the safety net for disaster recovery
choices.C: It immediately deletes all historical versions of the table and removes the table
choices.D: It enables 90-day Time Travel retention on the table automatically

questions.explanation: Setting retention to 0 disables Time Travel for the object (no AT/BEFORE or UNDROP recovery). For permanent tables, the 7-day Fail-safe still applies and cannot be disabled.

questions.question: Which best describes 'zero-copy cloning' with respect to data protection workflows?

choices.A: It duplicates data physically to multiple storage locations for immediate backup and redundancy across regions
choices.B: It quickly creates a logical, independent copy for testing/dev or point-in-time snapshots without extra storage until changes occur
choices.C: It only clones empty tables without any data; populated tables cannot be cloned or referenced
choices.D: It is slower than a full copy due to the overhead of maintaining logical independence across storage

questions.explanation: Zero-copy cloning instantly creates an independent logical copy sharing existing micro-partitions. It is ideal for dev/test environments and point-in-time snapshots, consuming new storage only as data diverges.

questions.question: Can you combine cloning with Time Travel to create a copy of a table as it existed in the past?

choices.A: No, cloning only works on current data; historical snapshots cannot be cloned
choices.B: Yes, CREATE TABLE ... CLONE ... AT/BEFORE lets you clone a historical state within retention
choices.C: Only with Fail-safe access and Snowflake support intervention for historical cloning
choices.D: Only for transient tables; permanent and temporary tables cannot be cloned with Time Travel

questions.explanation: You can clone an object at a historical point using CLONE ... AT(TIMESTAMP/OFFSET/STATEMENT) or BEFORE, as long as that point is within the Time Travel retention period.

questions.question: Which statement about sharing with a non-Snowflake customer is correct?

choices.A: It is impossible to share data with non-Snowflake customers using any Snowflake feature or mechanism
choices.B: The provider creates a reader account so the consumer can access shared data using a Snowflake-managed UI/compute paid by the provider
choices.C: The consumer must buy a full Snowflake account to access and query any shared data or explore databases
choices.D: Only CSV exports and file transfers are allowed for non-Snowflake customer sharing, no SQL access

questions.explanation: Reader accounts let providers share data with consumers who lack their own Snowflake account. The provider creates and pays for the reader account's compute and manages its users.

questions.question: What governance feature ensures masking is consistently applied to all columns tagged as sensitive?

choices.A: Manual masking configured individually on each column through ALTER TABLE statements
choices.B: Tag-based masking policies (attach a masking policy to a tag)
choices.C: Network policies that restrict access based on IP addresses and connection origins
choices.D: Resource monitors that track and limit compute resource allocation for sensitive data

questions.explanation: Tag-based masking policies let you assign a masking policy to a tag; any column with that tag automatically inherits the masking policy, ensuring consistent protection across the account.

questions.question: Which is true about querying shared data performance?

choices.A: The consumer's queries run on the provider's warehouse infrastructure and shared compute
choices.B: The consumer uses their own warehouse, so performance depends on the consumer's compute
choices.C: Shared data cannot be joined with the consumer's local data in any query scenario
choices.D: Shared data is always cached locally on the consumer's infrastructure for fast access

questions.explanation: Consumers query shared data using their own virtual warehouses; performance is governed by the consumer's compute. Shared data can be joined freely with the consumer's own data.

questions.question: Which approach lets a provider share data so that each consumer sees only their own rows from a shared table?

choices.A: A single non-secure view that allows all consumers to see all rows from the shared table
choices.B: Secure views with CURRENT_ACCOUNT()/CURRENT_ROLE() logic or row access policies to filter per-consumer
choices.C: Replicating separate tables per consumer manually and managing distinct shares
choices.D: It is not possible to implement row-level filtering in shared data scenarios

questions.explanation: Providers can use secure views referencing context functions (e.g., CURRENT_ACCOUNT) or row access policies and entitlement mapping tables so each consumer account sees only its permitted rows from a shared dataset.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: What does SnowCD (Snowflake Connectivity Diagnostic) help with?

choices.A: Assisting with data ingestion and bulk loading of files from cloud storage
choices.B: Diagnosing network connectivity issues between a client host and Snowflake endpoints
choices.C: Applying data masking and column-level security policies to sensitive tables
choices.D: Scheduling and orchestrating recurring task execution across warehouses

questions.explanation: SnowCD is a connectivity diagnostic tool that checks network access to Snowflake endpoints, helping troubleshoot firewall, proxy, and PrivateLink connectivity problems.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which connector/driver would a Python application typically use to connect to Snowflake?

choices.A: ODBC driver is the only supported method for Python applications
choices.B: The Snowflake Connector for Python (and optionally SQLAlchemy/Snowpark)
choices.C: JDBC drivers are the exclusive way to connect Python to Snowflake
choices.D: SnowSQL CLI library is the standard Python application connection method

questions.explanation: Python applications commonly use the Snowflake Connector for Python; higher-level options include the SQLAlchemy dialect and the Snowpark Python API for DataFrame-style processing.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which interface(s) can be used to administer and query Snowflake?

choices.A: Only the Snowsight web-based user interface for querying
choices.B: Snowsight web UI, SnowSQL CLI, drivers (JDBC/ODBC), connectors, and REST APIs
choices.C: Only the SnowSQL command-line interface without other access methods
choices.D: Only JDBC drivers for administrative and operational tasks

questions.explanation: Snowflake can be accessed via the Snowsight web UI, SnowSQL CLI, language connectors (Python, etc.), JDBC/ODBC drivers, the SQL REST API, and Snowpark, providing broad connectivity options.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: What distinguishes Virtual Private Snowflake (VPS) from Business Critical edition?

choices.A: Virtual Private Snowflake has significantly fewer features than other editions and reduced capabilities
choices.B: VPS provides a completely isolated Snowflake environment (dedicated metadata store and pool) for the highest security needs
choices.C: Virtual Private Snowflake removes encryption and reduces security controls for improved performance
choices.D: Virtual Private Snowflake is cheaper than Standard edition pricing while providing identical features

questions.explanation: VPS is the highest edition, offering a dedicated, isolated Snowflake environment (separate from other Snowflake customers) to meet the most stringent security and regulatory requirements.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is a correct statement about Snowflake's metadata?

choices.A: Metadata is stored and maintained within each virtual warehouse independently
choices.B: Metadata is managed centrally by the Cloud Services layer, enabling features like pruning and Time Travel
choices.C: Users must manually create and maintain all metadata definitions and statistics
choices.D: Metadata is stored exclusively in the user's local workstation cache

questions.explanation: The Cloud Services layer centrally manages metadata (micro-partition statistics, object definitions, history), which powers query optimization, pruning, Time Travel, and zero-copy cloning.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Why can Snowflake offer zero-copy cloning so efficiently?

choices.A: Clones physically compress data during the cloning operation to minimize storage consumption immediately
choices.B: Clones reference the same immutable micro-partitions via metadata pointers; no data is physically copied until changes occur
choices.C: Clones use a dedicated backup warehouse to store redundant copies of all cloned data and metadata
choices.D: Clones copy only the schema definition without any data content, which must be loaded separately

questions.explanation: Because micro-partitions are immutable and tracked by metadata, a clone simply creates new metadata pointers to the same partitions. Storage is consumed only when either copy is modified (copy-on-write).

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which statement about Snowflake's high availability is correct?

choices.A: Snowflake infrastructure runs on a single centralized server managed as a monolithic system
choices.B: Snowflake is a fully managed service designed for high availability across multiple availability zones within a region
choices.C: Customers are responsible for configuring and managing failover hardware across availability zones
choices.D: System availability depends entirely on the configured warehouse size and scaling configuration

questions.explanation: Snowflake is a fully managed SaaS that distributes its services across multiple availability zones within a cloud region for resilience and high availability, with cross-region replication available for DR.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which feature provides directory-style metadata about files in a stage, enabling querying of file listings?

choices.A: Directory table
choices.B: External table
choices.C: Stream
choices.D: Sequence

questions.explanation: A directory table is an implicit, queryable object layered on a stage that stores file-level metadata (path, size, last modified, etc.), often used with unstructured data and for triggering processing.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which capability supports storing and governing unstructured files (PDFs, images) in Snowflake?

choices.A: VARIANT columns are used to store and govern unstructured file objects
choices.B: Stages with directory tables and scoped/file URLs
choices.C: Sequence objects provide the primary mechanism for storing files
choices.D: The result cache mechanism provides file storage and governance

questions.explanation: Snowflake supports unstructured data via stages plus directory tables, and provides scoped URLs, file URLs, and pre-signed URLs (with functions like BUILD_SCOPED_FILE_URL) to access governed files.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which function generates a temporary, encoded URL to access a staged file with access enforced by Snowflake privileges?

choices.A: GET_PRESIGNED_URL
choices.B: BUILD_SCOPED_FILE_URL
choices.C: TO_FILE
choices.D: STAGE_URL

questions.explanation: BUILD_SCOPED_FILE_URL creates a scoped, time-limited URL whose access is governed by the caller's Snowflake privileges. GET_PRESIGNED_URL creates a pre-signed URL valid for a set duration that bypasses Snowflake auth.

questions.question: Which statement about applying a masking policy to a column is correct?

choices.A: A single column can have multiple masking policies applied simultaneously in a processing sequence
choices.B: A single masking policy can be applied to many columns, but each column can have only one masking policy
choices.C: Masking policies can only be applied to numeric and date columns, not text or other data types
choices.D: Masking policies permanently modify and overwrite stored column data at the storage layer

questions.explanation: A masking policy is reusable across multiple columns, but a given column can have at most one masking policy. Masking is applied at query time and does not change stored data.

questions.question: Within a masking policy definition, which context function is commonly used to decide whether to mask based on who is querying?

choices.A: CURRENT_ROLE() or CURRENT_USER()
choices.B: CURRENT_WAREHOUSE()
choices.C: CURRENT_DATABASE()
choices.D: CURRENT_REGION()

questions.explanation: Masking policies typically branch on CURRENT_ROLE() or invoker context functions (e.g., IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION) to return clear text for privileged roles and masked values for others.

questions.question: What does a conditional masking policy use to determine masking?

choices.A: Only the querying role's characteristics and attributes, without referencing any column values
choices.B: The value of another column in the same row, in addition to context
choices.C: Only the warehouse size and compute tier where the query is being executed
choices.D: Only the time of day and calendar date when the query executes, without contextual information

questions.explanation: Conditional masking policies can reference another column's value (passed as an argument) to decide whether/how to mask, enabling row-aware masking decisions.

questions.question: Which privilege is needed to create masking policies and row access policies?

choices.A: CREATE MASKING POLICY / CREATE ROW ACCESS POLICY on the schema
choices.B: USAGE on the warehouse, which permits query execution and warehouse resource management
choices.C: OPERATE on the database, which grants comprehensive operational control over database functions
choices.D: MONITOR on the account, which allows viewing and tracking of account-level metrics

questions.explanation: Creating policies requires the relevant CREATE <policy type> privilege on the schema (e.g., CREATE MASKING POLICY), typically managed by a security/governance role, plus APPLY to attach them.

questions.question: What is the PUBLIC role's relationship to custom roles?

choices.A: PUBLIC is the highest-level parent role from which all custom roles inherit automatically
choices.B: PUBLIC is automatically granted to all users/roles and sits at the bottom of the hierarchy
choices.C: PUBLIC cannot hold or own any objects within the account
choices.D: PUBLIC has all account privileges by default and functions as a system administrator role

questions.explanation: PUBLIC is the lowest-level role automatically held by everyone. Objects granted to or owned by PUBLIC are accessible to all users. It has minimal privileges by default.

questions.question: What is the difference between authentication and authorization in Snowflake?

choices.A: Authentication and authorization are interchangeable terms describing the same security function
choices.B: Authentication verifies identity (login); authorization determines what actions/objects the identity can access (RBAC)
choices.C: Authorization occurs at login, while authentication is verified after the session is established
choices.D: Authorization is managed by warehouses and is separate from user access control mechanisms

questions.explanation: Authentication confirms who you are (password, SSO, key pair, MFA), while authorization (via RBAC roles and privileges) governs what you are allowed to do once authenticated.

questions.question: Which command is used to assign a role to a user?

choices.A: GRANT ROLE <role> TO USER <user>
choices.B: ASSIGN ROLE
choices.C: ALTER USER SET ROLE
choices.D: USE ROLE

questions.explanation: GRANT ROLE <role_name> TO USER <user_name> assigns a role to a user. USE ROLE only activates a role within the current session; it does not grant it.

questions.question: What does setting a user's DEFAULT_ROLE accomplish?

choices.A: It grants the role to the user, automatically making them a member with all role privileges
choices.B: Specifies which already-granted role becomes active automatically when the user starts a session
choices.C: It removes all other previously granted roles, leaving only the specified default role
choices.D: It automatically elevates the user to account administrator with enhanced system privileges

questions.explanation: DEFAULT_ROLE sets which of the user's granted roles is activated by default at session start. The role must already be granted to the user; the default just selects the initial active role.

questions.question: Which is a security best practice for the number of users holding ACCOUNTADMIN?

choices.A: All users in the organization must hold ACCOUNTADMIN to maintain operational continuity
choices.B: At least two users should have it (to avoid lockout) but kept to a minimal trusted set
choices.C: Zero users should ever receive ACCOUNTADMIN for maximum security and isolation
choices.D: ACCOUNTADMIN should be granted to the PUBLIC role so all users have equal administrative access

questions.explanation: Snowflake recommends having at least two ACCOUNTADMIN users (to avoid being locked out) while keeping the total minimal, using MFA, and assigning real email addresses to those accounts.

questions.question: Which feature lets you enforce that certain roles cannot be activated together in a session, supporting separation of duties?

choices.A: Secondary roles automatically activate all granted roles simultaneously without any restrictions
choices.B: Careful role hierarchy design and restricting role grants; Snowflake evaluates privileges via active primary/secondary roles
choices.C: Network policies prevent certain roles from being combined in the same session
choices.D: Resource monitors automatically deactivate roles based on credit consumption to enforce separation

questions.explanation: Separation of duties is implemented through deliberate role design and limiting which roles a user is granted, since privileges derive from active roles. Avoid over-granting and structure roles to prevent conflicting combined privileges.

questions.question: Which is generally the best indicator that a warehouse should be scaled UP rather than out?

choices.A: Multiple queries queuing in the execution queue due to concurrency limits
choices.B: Individual complex queries are slow and show heavy disk spilling
choices.C: The warehouse remaining idle for extended periods during the day
choices.D: Low overall credit consumption relative to storage usage

questions.explanation: Slow individual queries with significant spilling indicate insufficient memory/CPU per query—scale up to a larger warehouse. Concurrency-driven queuing instead calls for scaling out (multi-cluster).

questions.question: Which monitoring view helps you analyze warehouse credit consumption over time?

choices.A: ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY
choices.B: INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES
choices.C: ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_ROLES
choices.D: SHOW WAREHOUSES

questions.explanation: WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY (in ACCOUNT_USAGE and INFORMATION_SCHEMA) reports credits consumed by each warehouse over time, supporting cost analysis and chargeback.

questions.question: What is a practical benefit of separating workloads onto different warehouses?

choices.A: It reduces the total amount of data storage required in the account or cloud buckets
choices.B: It isolates workloads so heavy jobs don't slow down others, and enables right-sizing and cost attribution per workload
choices.C: It disables Time Travel and historical recovery features for all objects in the system
choices.D: It eliminates the requirement for role-based access controls and security policies

questions.explanation: Dedicating warehouses to specific workloads (ETL, BI, data science) isolates performance, allows independent sizing/scaling, and makes cost attribution clearer.

questions.question: Which statement about materialized view costs is accurate?

choices.A: Materialized views incur no storage or compute charges of any kind in the billing system
choices.B: They incur storage for the stored results plus serverless compute for automatic maintenance as base data changes
choices.C: They incur only storage costs with no maintenance compute or serverless credits required
choices.D: They rely entirely on the result cache for cost optimization and performance improvement

questions.explanation: Materialized views cost storage (for materialized results) and serverless maintenance credits to keep them up to date as the underlying table changes. They suit stable data with expensive repeated queries.

questions.question: What does enabling the Search Optimization Service cost?

choices.A: Search Optimization Service is provided at no additional cost
choices.B: Storage for the search access path plus serverless compute to build and maintain it
choices.C: Only a one-time implementation fee regardless of usage
choices.D: Additional per-query warehouse credits when optimization is used

questions.explanation: Search Optimization adds storage for its search access paths and consumes serverless maintenance credits to build and keep them current. It pays off when many selective lookups are accelerated.

questions.question: Which factor does NOT directly increase Snowflake compute (credit) costs?

choices.A: Running larger warehouses
choices.B: Longer-running queries
choices.C: Storing more historical data (storage)
choices.D: More concurrent clusters running

questions.explanation: Storing more data increases storage costs, not compute credits. Compute costs are driven by warehouse size, runtime, and number of running clusters/serverless usage.

questions.question: Which setting helps prevent a single runaway query from consuming excessive credits?

choices.A: AUTO_RESUME
choices.B: STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS
choices.C: DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS
choices.D: MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT

questions.explanation: STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS caps how long any query can run before being canceled, protecting against runaway queries that would otherwise consume credits indefinitely.

questions.question: When does the local disk (SSD) cache most improve performance?

choices.A: Immediately on the first query executed after warehouse resumption
choices.B: On repeated queries against the same data while the warehouse stays running (warm cache)
choices.C: Exclusively for data definition language statements
choices.D: Only when the result cache is explicitly disabled
