questions.explanation: The warehouse SSD cache benefits repeated access to the same micro-partitions while the warehouse remains running. After suspend/resume the cache is cold and must be rebuilt.

questions.question: Which is a recommended way to control costs for ad-hoc analytics by many users?

choices.A: Provision each user with a dedicated 6XL warehouse for exclusive access
choices.B: Use a right-sized multi-cluster warehouse with auto-suspend and a resource monitor
choices.C: Disable AUTO_SUSPEND to keep warehouses continuously active
choices.D: Route all analytics queries through the main ETL warehouse

questions.explanation: A right-sized multi-cluster warehouse handles concurrency, auto-suspend avoids idle cost, and a resource monitor caps spend—together providing cost-controlled ad-hoc analytics.

questions.question: A query scans far more partitions than expected despite a selective WHERE clause on column C. What is a likely cause?

choices.A: The result cache is enabled and returning stale results from previous query executions
choices.B: Data is not clustered by C, so values are spread across many micro-partitions, limiting pruning
choices.C: The warehouse compute size exceeds the actual requirements causing inefficient query execution
choices.D: Time Travel is enabled with extended retention periods creating additional metadata overhead

questions.explanation: If the table isn't clustered (naturally or via a clustering key) by column C, matching values appear in many micro-partitions, so pruning can't eliminate them. Clustering or Search Optimization on C would help.

questions.question: Which is true about the relationship between a pipe and a COPY statement?

choices.A: A pipe contains no COPY statement and cannot load data
choices.B: A pipe wraps a COPY INTO statement that Snowpipe runs automatically as files arrive
choices.C: A pipe replaces the stage entirely, eliminating cloud storage
choices.D: A pipe is configured only to unload data, not load it

questions.explanation: A pipe object encapsulates a COPY INTO <table> statement. Snowpipe executes this COPY automatically (via notifications or REST API) to load new files continuously.

questions.question: How long does Snowpipe retain load history used for duplicate detection?

choices.A: 24 hours
choices.B: 14 days
choices.C: 64 days
choices.D: 365 days

questions.explanation: Snowpipe retains load metadata to avoid reloading the same file for 14 days. (Bulk COPY duplicate-detection metadata is retained for 64 days.)

questions.question: Which approach should you use to load data continuously with the lowest latency?

choices.A: Execute manual COPY once daily to load all available files
choices.B: Snowpipe with auto-ingest (or Snowpipe Streaming for row-level)
choices.C: Use INSERT statements in a loop to add rows without staging
choices.D: Query external tables repeatedly as a proxy for ingestion

questions.explanation: Snowpipe auto-ingest provides continuous micro-batch loading shortly after files arrive. For even lower latency at the row level, Snowpipe Streaming ingests rows directly without staging files.

questions.question: What is a key difference between Snowpipe Streaming and classic Snowpipe?

choices.A: Snowpipe Streaming requires staging files first before writing rows into target tables
choices.B: Snowpipe Streaming writes rows directly into Snowflake via an API, avoiding intermediate files for lower latency
choices.C: Classic Snowpipe writes rows directly via API without requiring intermediate file staging
choices.D: Snowpipe Streaming and classic Snowpipe have identical functionality and capabilities

questions.explanation: Snowpipe Streaming uses an SDK to write rows directly into target tables without first staging files, achieving lower latency and often lower cost for streaming row data compared to file-based classic Snowpipe.

questions.question: Which file format property would you set to handle fields enclosed in double quotes in a CSV?

choices.A: FIELD_OPTIONALLY_ENCLOSED_BY
choices.B: RECORD_DELIMITER
choices.C: COMPRESSION
choices.D: DATE_FORMAT

questions.explanation: FIELD_OPTIONALLY_ENCLOSED_BY specifies the character (e.g., a double quote) used to optionally enclose field values, allowing delimiters within quoted fields to be handled correctly.

questions.question: When unloading to a single Parquet file, which consideration is important?

choices.A: Parquet cannot be used as an output format for unload operations under any circumstances, so it must be converted first
choices.B: Use the appropriate file format type = PARQUET; very large single files may hit size limits, so partitioning may be preferable
choices.C: Parquet data must first be loaded into CSV and then unloaded to Parquet to guarantee that the file format stays compatible
choices.D: The SINGLE = TRUE parameter is incompatible with Parquet output, so single-file Parquet unloads are not permitted at all

questions.explanation: You can unload to Parquet by specifying TYPE = PARQUET in the file format. For very large outputs, allowing multiple files (or partitioning with PARTITION BY) is often better than forcing one huge file.

questions.question: Which COPY INTO <location> option organizes unloaded files into directories based on column values?

choices.A: PARTITION BY
choices.B: CLUSTER BY
choices.C: GROUP BY
choices.D: ORDER BY

questions.explanation: The PARTITION BY clause in COPY INTO <location> writes unloaded data into a partitioned directory structure based on the specified expression, useful for data lake organization.

questions.question: What is a benefit of using external tables with partitioning over loading all external data into Snowflake?

choices.A: External tables execute faster than native tables in all cases without exception
choices.B: You can query data in place (no ingestion/storage cost in Snowflake) and prune by partition columns
choices.C: External tables support full DML operations like INSERT and UPDATE on data in cloud storage
choices.D: External table metadata never requires refreshing automatically as files change

questions.explanation: External tables let you query data residing in cloud storage without loading it into Snowflake (saving storage/ingest), and partitioning enables pruning. They are read-only and metadata may need refreshing as files change.

questions.question: Which command refreshes the metadata of an external table to recognize new files?

choices.A: ALTER EXTERNAL TABLE ... REFRESH
choices.B: REFRESH STAGE
choices.C: COPY INTO
choices.D: RELOAD TABLE

questions.explanation: ALTER EXTERNAL TABLE <name> REFRESH updates the external table's metadata to detect added/removed files. Auto-refresh via cloud notifications can also keep it current.

questions.question: Which is a valid reason to materialize a view over an external table?

choices.A: External tables cannot be queried and require a view intermediary to access any data
choices.B: A materialized view over an external table can pre-compute results and improve query performance on slow external data
choices.C: A materialized view enables writing results to external storage without any limitations
choices.D: Creating a materialized view disables partition pruning and optimization completely

questions.explanation: Creating a materialized view on an external table can significantly speed up repeated queries by pre-computing and storing results in Snowflake, mitigating the slower performance of scanning external files.

questions.question: Which clause is required to use a window function?

choices.A: GROUP BY
choices.B: OVER (...)
choices.C: HAVING
choices.D: PARTITION TABLE

questions.explanation: Window functions use the OVER clause to define the window (optional PARTITION BY and ORDER BY). Unlike GROUP BY, window functions retain individual rows while computing across the window.

questions.question: What does LAG() do in a windowed query?

choices.A: Returns the value from a following row within the window
choices.B: Returns the value from a preceding row within the window
choices.C: Returns the cumulative running total of all prior rows
choices.D: Returns the maximum value in the entire window partition

questions.explanation: LAG() accesses a value from a previous row (offset back) within the ordered window. LEAD() accesses a value from a following row.

questions.question: Which function would you use to remove leading and trailing spaces from a string?

choices.A: TRIM
choices.B: REPLACE
choices.C: SUBSTRING
choices.D: LENGTH

questions.explanation: TRIM removes leading and trailing characters (spaces by default). LTRIM and RTRIM handle only the left or right side respectively.

questions.question: Which is correct about how Snowflake handles case sensitivity for unquoted identifiers?

choices.A: Unquoted identifiers are stored and resolved as lowercase
choices.B: Unquoted identifiers are stored and resolved as uppercase by default
choices.C: All identifiers are case-sensitive regardless of quoting
choices.D: Identifiers cannot contain alphabetic characters

questions.explanation: Snowflake converts unquoted identifiers to uppercase. Double-quoted identifiers preserve case and become case-sensitive, so 'mytable' and "MyTable" can differ.

questions.question: Which function concatenates strings together?

choices.A: CONCAT or the || operator
choices.B: SPLIT
choices.C: ARRAY_AGG
choices.D: PARSE_JSON

questions.explanation: CONCAT(a, b, ...) or the || operator concatenates string values. CONCAT_WS adds a separator between values.

questions.question: What does the DATEADD function return?

choices.A: Calculates the difference in days between two dates
choices.B: A new date/timestamp with a specified interval added to an input date
choices.C: Returns the current system date and timestamp
choices.D: Converts a date into string representation format

questions.explanation: DATEADD(part, value, date) adds the specified interval (e.g., days, months) to a date/timestamp and returns the result. DATEDIFF computes the difference between two dates.

questions.question: Which statement creates a view that hides its definition from unauthorized users?

choices.A: CREATE SECURE VIEW
choices.B: CREATE HIDDEN VIEW
choices.C: CREATE PRIVATE VIEW
choices.D: CREATE MASKED VIEW

questions.explanation: CREATE SECURE VIEW defines a secure view whose definition is hidden from users without ownership and which bypasses certain optimizations to protect underlying data.

questions.question: Which is true about transient databases and schemas regarding contained objects?

choices.A: Transient databases can contain permanent tables with full Fail-safe protection
choices.B: Objects created within a transient database/schema are transient by default (no Fail-safe)
choices.C: Transient databases cannot contain any table objects
choices.D: Transient objects automatically delete after a 24-hour period

questions.explanation: If a database or schema is transient, tables created within it are transient by default, inheriting the no-Fail-safe characteristic. You cannot create a permanent table inside a transient container.

questions.question: Which function splits a delimited string into an ARRAY?

choices.A: SPLIT
choices.B: FLATTEN
choices.C: CONCAT
choices.D: TRIM

questions.explanation: SPLIT(string, delimiter) divides a string into an ARRAY of substrings based on the delimiter. SPLIT_PART returns a single part by index.

questions.question: Which best describes the behavior of a UDF marked as IMMUTABLE/deterministic for the same inputs?

choices.A: An immutable function returns different results for identical inputs
choices.B: It returns the same output for the same inputs, enabling optimizations like caching
choices.C: An immutable function cannot be used in SELECT statements
choices.D: An immutable function must query external systems for each execution

questions.explanation: A deterministic (immutable) function consistently returns the same result for identical inputs, allowing Snowflake to optimize and potentially reuse results. Non-deterministic functions (e.g., RANDOM) cannot be cached this way.

questions.question: Which statement about a database created from a share (inbound share) is correct?

choices.A: It consumes the consumer's storage equal to the full size of the shared data
choices.B: It is a read-only database referencing the provider's data, consuming no consumer storage
choices.C: It can be fully modified by the consumer; the database is writable
choices.D: It must be replicated to the consumer's account before it can be used

questions.explanation: A shared (inbound) database is read-only and points to the provider's data; the consumer does not store a copy and incurs no storage cost for it—only compute when querying.

questions.question: What happens to a consumer's access if the provider removes an object from the share?

choices.A: The consumer keeps a cached copy
choices.B: The consumer immediately loses access to that object
choices.C: Access continues for 24 hours
choices.D: The object is copied to the consumer

questions.explanation: Because sharing provides live, real-time access, revoking an object from a share immediately removes the consumer's ability to query that object—no cached copy persists.

questions.question: Which is a benefit of Secure Data Sharing over traditional data exchange (e.g., file transfer)?

choices.A: Requires building custom ETL pipelines and data transformation workflows
choices.B: No copying/ETL; consumers access live, always-current data instantly and securely
choices.C: Data becomes stale immediately after each query due to caching limitations
choices.D: Higher storage costs for consumers who maintain local copies of shared data

questions.explanation: Secure Data Sharing eliminates copying and ETL: consumers query the provider's live data directly, always seeing current data, with strong security and no duplicate storage.

questions.question: Which object types can a provider include in a share to expose computed logic securely?

choices.A: Standard (non-secure) views and user-defined functions with visible definitions
choices.B: Secure views, secure materialized views, and secure UDFs
choices.C: Tasks and streams for event-driven data processing and transformation logic
choices.D: Warehouses and compute resources that consumers can directly utilize

questions.explanation: To expose logic without revealing definitions or underlying data, providers share secure views, secure materialized views, and secure UDFs. Non-secure objects would risk exposing internal details.

questions.question: What is required to share data across cloud regions using Marketplace listings?

choices.A: Manual file export and transfer to the target region by the provider
choices.B: Cross-cloud auto-fulfillment, which replicates the data to the target region behind the scenes
choices.C: A reader account must be created in each target region before listing activation
choices.D: Standard edition subscription is required in all regions for cross-region listings

questions.explanation: Listings support cross-cloud/region auto-fulfillment, automatically replicating the underlying data to the consumer's region so they can access it locally without the provider manually managing replication.

questions.question: Which is true about Time Travel on a table that has had its retention reduced from 30 to 1 day?

choices.A: Historical data older than the new retention becomes inaccessible going forward
choices.B: All historical data is permanently retained regardless of the new retention setting
choices.C: The change has no effect; retention adjustments do not affect accessibility
choices.D: It extends the Fail-safe period to 30 days to compensate for reduced Time Travel

questions.explanation: Reducing retention shortens how far back you can Time Travel; data beyond the new, shorter window is no longer accessible (and moves toward Fail-safe/purge). Increasing retention only affects data going forward and currently within range.

questions.question: Which command would restore a table to a previous state using Time Travel by recreating it?

choices.A: CREATE TABLE ... CLONE ... BEFORE (STATEMENT => '<query_id>')
choices.B: ROLLBACK statement to revert the table to its previous state
choices.C: UNDROP TABLE command, which recovers dropped tables to their current state
choices.D: ALTER TABLE RESTORE command to restore the table to a previous version

questions.explanation: You can recreate or clone a table at a prior point using CLONE with AT/BEFORE (timestamp, offset, or statement id). UNDROP only recovers a dropped object, not a logical state change.

questions.question: Which Snowflake capability supports compliance by retaining query and access history for auditing?

choices.A: Result cache that stores and reports historical query results
choices.B: ACCOUNT_USAGE views (e.g., QUERY_HISTORY, ACCESS_HISTORY, LOGIN_HISTORY)
choices.C: Materialized views that track all account activity and access patterns
choices.D: Sequences that generate unique identifiers for audit trail records

questions.explanation: ACCOUNT_USAGE views like QUERY_HISTORY, LOGIN_HISTORY, and ACCESS_HISTORY (which tracks column-level data access) provide up to 365 days of history for auditing and compliance reporting.

questions.question: What does the ACCESS_HISTORY view provide that QUERY_HISTORY does not?

choices.A: Warehouse credit usage and compute consumption per query or user session
choices.B: Column-level data access lineage (which columns/objects each query read or wrote)
choices.C: Login attempts and user authentication events for security auditing
choices.D: Storage metrics and account-level data volume tracking and reporting

questions.explanation: ACCESS_HISTORY records fine-grained, column-level read/write access (data lineage) per query, supporting governance, auditing, and impact analysis beyond what QUERY_HISTORY offers.

questions.question: Which feature would you use to ensure sensitive data shared with a partner is masked unless they have an authorized role?

choices.A: Standard view that shows all values without any masking or filtering
choices.B: Masking policy applied to the shared columns (in combination with secure views)
choices.C: Resource monitor to track and limit resource consumption for sensitive data
choices.D: Result cache configuration to optimize access to sensitive information

questions.explanation: Applying masking policies to columns (often via secure views in the share) ensures consumers see masked values unless their context/role is authorized, preserving privacy in data sharing scenarios.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which statement about Snowflake's storage compression is correct?

choices.A: Customers select and manage the specific compression algorithm applied
choices.B: Snowflake automatically compresses data and customers are billed on the compressed size
choices.C: All data is stored uncompressed for maximum query performance
choices.D: Compression is applied only to semi-structured VARIANT data, not structured tables

questions.explanation: Snowflake automatically applies columnar compression to all stored data; customers are billed based on the compressed storage size, and the compression is fully managed by Snowflake.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is the correct order of object containment in Snowflake?

choices.A: Account > Schema > Database > Table
choices.B: Account > Database > Schema > Table
choices.C: Database > Account > Schema > Table
choices.D: Account > Table > Schema > Database

questions.explanation: The containment hierarchy is Account > Database > Schema > Table (and other schema-level objects like views, stages, and pipes).

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: What is an account in Snowflake?

choices.A: A single individual user login credential within Snowflake
choices.B: An independent Snowflake deployment with its own URL, users, roles, databases, and warehouses
choices.C: A warehouse configuration type for specialized compute workloads
choices.D: A schema-level object for organizing tables and views

questions.explanation: A Snowflake account is an isolated instance with its own identifier/URL, hosting users, roles, databases, warehouses, and configuration. Multiple accounts can belong to one organization.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which scenario best illustrates the benefit of separating storage and compute?

choices.A: All queries must be shut down before adding storage capacity to the account
choices.B: An ETL warehouse and a BI warehouse query the same tables concurrently and are sized/billed independently
choices.C: Storage capacity can only scale when compute resources are added or modified
choices.D: Only a single warehouse can run queries at any given moment in time

questions.explanation: Separation of storage and compute lets multiple independent warehouses access the same shared data simultaneously, each sized and billed separately—enabling workload isolation without data duplication.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which best describes 'pruning' in Snowflake query processing?

choices.A: Automatically deleting old historical data beyond a retention period without recovery
choices.B: Skipping micro-partitions that cannot contain rows matching the query filters, using partition metadata
choices.C: Removing unused or unreferenced columns from table definitions during query execution
choices.D: Compressing and optimizing the format of query result sets for faster network transmission

questions.explanation: Pruning uses per-micro-partition metadata (min/max, etc.) to skip partitions that cannot match the query's predicates, reducing the amount of data scanned and improving performance.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which factor most influences how well Snowflake can prune micro-partitions for a query?

choices.A: The selected virtual warehouse size directly influences pruning effectiveness
choices.B: How well the table's data is clustered/ordered relative to the filtered columns
choices.C: The result cache warmth determines how well pruning operates
choices.D: The total number of concurrent users accessing tables affects pruning

questions.explanation: Pruning effectiveness depends on data organization: if the filtered column's values are well-clustered, fewer partitions need scanning. Poor clustering spreads values across many partitions, reducing pruning.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which Snowflake object is schema-level rather than account-level?

choices.A: Warehouse
choices.B: Table
choices.C: Role
choices.D: User

questions.explanation: Tables (and views, stages, pipes, sequences, etc.) are schema-level objects. Warehouses, roles, and users are account-level objects.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: What is the role of the optimizer in the Cloud Services layer?

choices.A: To persistently store and manage the table data itself within the Cloud Services layer alongside the system metadata
choices.B: To parse SQL and generate an efficient execution plan, including pruning decisions, before dispatching to a warehouse
choices.C: To calculate and bill the customer for credit consumption based on how long each virtual warehouse stays running
choices.D: To manage and maintain the local SSD cache pools on the warehouse compute nodes that hold recently scanned data

questions.explanation: The Cloud Services optimizer parses and compiles SQL, applies cost-based optimization (join ordering, pruning, etc.), and produces a plan that the virtual warehouse executes.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is true about a suspended virtual warehouse?

choices.A: It still consumes compute credits
choices.B: It consumes no compute credits while suspended
choices.C: It deletes the table data
choices.D: It cannot be resumed

questions.explanation: A suspended warehouse consumes no compute credits. Storage costs continue (they're independent of warehouses), but you only pay compute when a warehouse is running.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which feature allows running Python, Java, or Scala code inside Snowflake's secure sandbox close to the data?

choices.A: External functions are the only mechanism for running application code
choices.B: Snowpark (including Snowpark-optimized warehouses and UDFs/procedures)
choices.C: SnowSQL CLI is used to execute Python, Java, or Scala code in Snowflake
choices.D: The result cache provides execution of application code written in Python

questions.explanation: Snowpark runs Python/Java/Scala logic within Snowflake's secure sandbox. Snowpark-optimized warehouses provide additional memory for demanding workloads like ML, keeping computation close to the data.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which best describes an organization in Snowflake?

choices.A: A single database object that contains tables and schemas within a specific warehouse
choices.B: A first-class entity that groups and manages multiple Snowflake accounts under unified administration
choices.C: A role-based access control construct within a Snowflake account for permission management
choices.D: A cluster of virtual warehouses configured for workload management and load balancing

questions.explanation: An organization links multiple Snowflake accounts for centralized management, enabling cross-account features like replication, failover, and consolidated usage via ORGANIZATION_USAGE, managed by ORGADMIN.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which statement about Snowflake's support for ANSI SQL is correct?

choices.A: Snowflake uses a proprietary query language instead of SQL
choices.B: Snowflake supports standard ANSI SQL with extensions for semi-structured data and other features
choices.C: Snowflake supports only NoSQL query patterns and document-oriented access
choices.D: Snowflake requires MapReduce code for distributed query execution

questions.explanation: Snowflake supports standard ANSI SQL plus extensions (e.g., FLATTEN, VARIANT path access, QUALIFY), making it accessible to SQL practitioners while adding modern capabilities.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which cache would help a dashboard that repeatedly runs the exact same query on unchanged data?

choices.A: Local disk cache
choices.B: Result cache
choices.C: Metadata cache
choices.D: No cache helps

questions.explanation: Identical queries on unchanged data are served from the result cache (up to 24 hours), returning instantly without using a warehouse—ideal for repeated dashboard queries.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Why is Snowflake described as a 'data cloud' beyond just a data warehouse?

choices.A: Because it exclusively stores unstructured file objects and documents and offers no SQL access to structured analytic data
choices.B: Because it unifies data warehousing, data lakes, data sharing/collaboration, data engineering, apps, and AI/ML on one platform
choices.C: Because it runs on only one cloud provider's infrastructure and is therefore unable to span multiple clouds or regions
choices.D: Because it completely replaces and eliminates every other cloud provider and service a customer might otherwise use

questions.explanation: The 'AI Data Cloud' encompasses multiple workloads—data warehouse, data lake, engineering, sharing/collaboration, applications, and AI/ML (Cortex)—across multiple clouds, going beyond a traditional data warehouse.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is true about how many schemas a database can contain?

choices.A: Exactly one schema is created by default in each database
choices.B: Many schemas, each containing tables and other objects
choices.C: Only two predefined schemas exist: PUBLIC and INFORMATION_SCHEMA
choices.D: Database objects cannot contain any schemas in their structure

questions.explanation: A database can contain many schemas. By default it includes PUBLIC and INFORMATION_SCHEMA, and you can create additional schemas to organize objects.

questions.question: Which command activates a different role within the current session?

choices.A: GRANT ROLE
choices.B: USE ROLE
choices.C: SET ROLE
choices.D: ALTER SESSION

questions.explanation: USE ROLE <role_name> switches the active (primary) role for the current session, provided the role has been granted to the user.

questions.question: Which is true about secondary roles in a session?

choices.A: Secondary roles completely replace the primary role, deactivating it for the session duration
choices.B: They allow a session to use privileges from additional granted roles alongside the primary role
choices.C: Secondary roles are always all roles by default, activated automatically without explicit action
choices.D: Secondary roles consist exclusively of system-predefined roles like SYSADMIN and SECURITYADMIN

questions.explanation: Secondary roles (USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL) let a session combine privileges from multiple granted roles in addition to the primary role, useful when queries span objects owned by different roles.

questions.question: Which privilege allows a role to read data from a table?

choices.A: SELECT
choices.B: USAGE
choices.C: OPERATE
choices.D: MONITOR

questions.explanation: The SELECT privilege on a table (plus USAGE on the parent database and schema) allows a role to query the table's data.

questions.question: What is the minimum set of privileges a role needs to query a table in another schema?

choices.A: SELECT on the table only; container privileges are inherited implicitly from parent objects
choices.B: USAGE on the database, USAGE on the schema, and SELECT on the table
choices.C: OWNERSHIP of the parent database, which grants all necessary schema and table permissions
choices.D: Membership in ACCOUNTADMIN, which bypasses all granular privilege verification checks

questions.explanation: Privileges are hierarchical: to query the table you need USAGE to traverse the database and schema containers plus SELECT on the table itself.

questions.question: Which is a recommended practice for managing access to many objects with minimal grants?

choices.A: Grant privileges directly to individual users while avoiding role-based approaches for clarity
choices.B: Use functional roles and future grants, and build a role hierarchy
choices.C: Use the PUBLIC role exclusively and assign all users identical permissions for simplicity
choices.D: Disable Role-Based Access Control entirely and implement alternative authorization mechanisms

questions.explanation: Best practice is to design functional/access roles, build a hierarchy, and use future grants so newly created objects automatically inherit privileges—minimizing repetitive manual grants.

questions.question: Which is true about MFA enrollment in Snowflake?

choices.A: MFA enrollment is automatically enforced for all account users without exception at signup
choices.B: Users self-enroll (via Duo), and admins can require/strongly encourage it, especially for privileged roles
choices.C: MFA requires a separate commercial license and is not included in standard Snowflake editions
choices.D: MFA functions exclusively with SSO providers and cannot be used with native Snowflake authentication

questions.explanation: Snowflake MFA (powered by Duo) is available to all users who self-enroll. Administrators are advised to require MFA particularly for ACCOUNTADMIN and other privileged users for stronger security.

questions.question: Which is the purpose of the MANAGE GRANTS privilege?

choices.A: To create and manage virtual warehouse configurations and auto-scaling compute policies
choices.B: To grant or revoke privileges on objects the role does not own
choices.C: To execute any query regardless of object ownership or applied privilege restrictions
choices.D: To control warehouse suspension timing and resume operations to manage credit consumption

questions.explanation: MANAGE GRANTS allows a role (like SECURITYADMIN) to grant and revoke privileges on objects across the account, even those it does not own, centralizing grant administration.

questions.question: Which approach helps comply with a requirement that data engineers can manage pipelines but cannot view production PII?

choices.A: Grant ACCOUNTADMIN to all data engineers to provide complete operational flexibility
choices.B: Use RBAC to grant pipeline/object privileges while applying masking policies that mask PII for the engineering role
choices.C: Disable Role-Based Access Control entirely to eliminate privilege-based restrictions
choices.D: Store PII data in a completely separate Snowflake account accessible only to administrators

questions.explanation: Combine RBAC (granting needed operational privileges) with dynamic data masking so the engineering role can manage pipelines but sees masked PII values, satisfying least-privilege and data-protection requirements.

questions.question: Which command would you use to see what roles have been granted to a user?

choices.A: SHOW GRANTS TO USER <user>
choices.B: SHOW ROLES
choices.C: DESCRIBE USER
choices.D: LIST ROLES

questions.explanation: SHOW GRANTS TO USER <user_name> lists the roles granted to that user. SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE shows privileges of a role.

questions.question: What is a recommended pattern for separating object-access roles from functional (business) roles?

choices.A: Consolidate all privileges into a single role and grant it directly to all users
choices.B: Create access roles holding object privileges, grant them to functional roles, and grant functional roles to users
choices.C: Grant privileges exclusively to PUBLIC and avoid creating custom role structures
choices.D: Avoid using roles entirely and grant object privileges directly to individual users

questions.explanation: A common best practice separates 'access roles' (which hold specific object privileges) from 'functional roles' (aligned to job functions). Access roles are granted to functional roles, which are granted to users—improving manageability.

questions.question: Which factor is NOT part of a virtual warehouse's compute resources?

choices.A: CPU
choices.B: Memory
choices.C: Temporary/local SSD storage
choices.D: Long-term table storage

questions.explanation: A warehouse provides CPU, memory, and temporary local SSD storage for query processing. Long-term table storage is part of the separate Database Storage layer, not the warehouse.

questions.question: Which scenario indicates that increasing the warehouse size will likely NOT help much?

choices.A: A query with significant data spilling to remote storage will always benefit from scaling
choices.B: A simple, highly selective single-row lookup that already prunes well and runs in milliseconds
choices.C: A large aggregation encountering memory exhaustion issues that cannot be addressed by sizing
choices.D: A complex multi-join query experiencing memory bottleneck and resource contention

questions.explanation: A tiny, well-pruned lookup is not compute-bound, so a bigger warehouse adds cost without meaningful speedup. Larger warehouses help compute-heavy queries that spill or need more parallelism/memory.

questions.question: Which is a benefit of setting AUTO_SUSPEND to a reasonable value like 60 seconds for an interactive warehouse?

choices.A: It maintains the cache in a warm state indefinitely without any cost implications
choices.B: It balances avoiding idle costs with retaining the cache briefly between bursts of activity
choices.C: It disables billing for the warehouse completely, eliminating all credit consumption
choices.D: It prevents query execution on the warehouse, stopping all user transactions

questions.explanation: A modest AUTO_SUSPEND (e.g., 60s) suspends quickly when truly idle while keeping the warehouse warm during short gaps in interactive use, balancing cost savings with cache reuse.

questions.question: Which combination would BEST optimize a workload of many concurrent, selective lookups on a huge table?

choices.A: A single XL single-cluster warehouse configuration for all concurrent lookup queries
choices.B: Search Optimization Service on the lookup columns plus a multi-cluster warehouse for concurrency
choices.C: Disable all caching mechanisms and use 6XL configuration for maximum performance
choices.D: A clustering key defined on low-cardinality columns only for selective point lookups

questions.explanation: Selective point lookups benefit from Search Optimization (fast pruning), while many concurrent users benefit from a multi-cluster warehouse to avoid queuing—together optimizing both per-query and concurrency performance cost-effectively.

questions.question: Which metric would you check to see if a warehouse is frequently queuing queries?

choices.A: Total bytes scanned in the most recent query executions and cumulative I/O metrics
choices.B: Queued overload time / queued provisioning time in query history or warehouse load
choices.C: The physical storage size of the warehouse and its allocated memory capacity
choices.D: The total number of databases in the account and schema counts per database

questions.explanation: Query history and the warehouse load chart expose queuing metrics (queued overload/provisioning time). High queuing indicates a need to scale out (multi-cluster) or otherwise add capacity.

questions.question: Which is true about the cost relationship of a multi-cluster warehouse?

choices.A: Multi-cluster configuration has identical costs to single-cluster warehouses
choices.B: You pay for each active cluster's credits; more running clusters means proportionally more credits
choices.C: Each additional cluster adds no cost to the billing and provides free compute resources
choices.D: Warehouse cost is determined entirely by storage consumption, not active cluster count

questions.explanation: A multi-cluster warehouse bills credits for each running cluster at the warehouse's size rate. Auto-scaling adds clusters (and cost) under load and removes them when demand drops to control spend.

questions.question: Which is the most direct way to identify which queries scanned the most bytes last week?

choices.A: Query ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY ordered by BYTES_SCANNED
choices.B: Execute SHOW TABLES command to identify heavy scan queries
choices.C: Use DESCRIBE WAREHOUSE to analyze historical query performance
choices.D: List stage files to identify queries with large external scans

questions.explanation: Querying QUERY_HISTORY and sorting by BYTES_SCANNED (and execution time) reveals the heaviest queries, which are prime candidates for optimization (clustering, rewriting, or right-sizing).

questions.question: Which approach reduces cost for an infrequently used but occasionally heavy reporting workload?

choices.A: Keep a large warehouse running continuously throughout the day for immediate availability
choices.B: Use a warehouse with auto-suspend/auto-resume so it only runs (and bills) when reports are executed
choices.C: Disable the auto-resume feature on the warehouse to prevent unexpected activation
choices.D: Execute all reports on the smallest warehouse size available to minimize costs

questions.explanation: Auto-suspend/auto-resume ensures the warehouse is only consuming credits during actual report execution, avoiding idle cost for an intermittent workload while still being available on demand.

questions.question: What is the effect of the result cache on cost for repeated identical queries?

choices.A: The result cache increases the overall compute cost
choices.B: It can serve results without a warehouse, avoiding compute cost for those repeats
choices.C: The result cache has no impact on query costs
choices.D: Each result cache hit incurs an additional per-hit fee

questions.explanation: Result cache hits return data from Cloud Services without running a warehouse, so repeated identical queries on unchanged data incur no compute cost.

questions.question: A team complains their queries slow down every morning when the warehouse first resumes. What is the likely cause and remedy?

choices.A: The storage limit on the warehouse has been reached, and allocating additional storage capacity each morning resolves the slowdown
choices.B: Cold SSD cache after resume; the first queries rebuild the cache. Consider a keep-warm strategy or accept brief warm-up
choices.C: The result cache has been disabled on the account, and re-enabling it would remove the warm-up delay seen after each resume
choices.D: Too many roles are defined in the account, and pruning unused roles each morning restores the warehouse to full query speed

questions.explanation: After suspension, the local SSD cache is cleared, so the first queries after resume run colder. Options include scheduling a warm-up query, increasing AUTO_SUSPEND, or accepting the brief initial slowdown.

questions.question: Which is true about loading a CSV with extra columns not present in the target table?

choices.A: CSV files with extra columns always load successfully, and Snowflake silently ignores the additional fields without any error
choices.B: You can select specific columns via a transformation, or it may error depending on options like ERROR_ON_COLUMN_COUNT_MISMATCH
choices.C: Extra columns found in the CSV are automatically appended to the target table definition without any validation or schema check
choices.D: A CSV file is not permitted to contain more columns than the target table defines, so such a file is rejected before parsing

questions.explanation: By default, ERROR_ON_COLUMN_COUNT_MISMATCH causes an error if the number of columns differs. You can disable it or use a SELECT transformation in COPY to map only the desired columns.

questions.question: Which strategy avoids reloading already-processed files while still detecting genuinely new files?

choices.A: Always use FORCE = TRUE to re-evaluate all files each time and force processing
choices.B: Rely on Snowflake's default load history (skip already-loaded files) and avoid FORCE unless intentional
choices.C: Delete and recreate the table before every load for clean state and fresh data
choices.D: Disable load metadata tracking to reload all files each time and detect changes

questions.explanation: Snowflake's default behavior tracks load history and skips files already loaded, so simply running COPY again loads only new files. FORCE = TRUE overrides this and risks duplicates.

questions.question: Which is required to load data from a private S3 bucket using an external stage?

choices.A: The bucket must have public ACL settings for Snowflake access
choices.B: Credentials or a storage integration granting Snowflake access to the bucket
choices.C: No credentials or permissions are required for any bucket
choices.D: Access to private buckets requires creating a reader account

questions.explanation: To read from a private bucket, Snowflake needs authorization—either inline credentials or, preferably, a storage integration that trusts a cloud IAM role granting access to the bucket.

questions.question: Which is a valid way to handle a load where some rows have data errors but you want to load the good rows?

choices.A: ON_ERROR = ABORT_STATEMENT skips rows and continues loading
choices.B: ON_ERROR = CONTINUE to skip bad rows and load the rest
choices.C: Use FORCE = TRUE to skip errors and load valid rows
choices.D: Use PURGE = TRUE to remove bad rows and load the rest

questions.explanation: ON_ERROR = CONTINUE skips individual erroneous rows and loads the remaining valid rows. You can then review skipped rows via COPY_HISTORY/validation to address the errors.

questions.question: Which command can validate and return all errors for files without loading them?

choices.A: COPY INTO ... VALIDATION_MODE = RETURN_ALL_ERRORS
choices.B: LIST @stage enumerates files and validates format/structure
choices.C: Using PUT to validate file formats before table loading
choices.D: Using GET to download and validate files on the client

questions.explanation: Running COPY with VALIDATION_MODE = RETURN_ALL_ERRORS validates the staged files and returns all errors encountered, without loading any data—useful for pre-load checks.

questions.question: What is a benefit of using a named stage over a table or user stage?

choices.A: Named stages cannot be reused and are bound to a single table, limiting them to loading data for that one table only
choices.B: Named stages are flexible, reusable across tables/users, and can have associated file formats and access controls
choices.C: Named stages are designated strictly for unloading data out of Snowflake and cannot be used as a source for loading
choices.D: Named stages bypass the account's security model and access controls entirely, granting open access to any role

questions.explanation: A named (internal or external) stage is a first-class object that can be reused across multiple tables and users, can have a default file format, and supports granular privileges—more flexible than table or user stages.

questions.question: Which is true about loading JSON where each file contains one large array of records?

choices.A: A large JSON array cannot be loaded into Snowflake at all
choices.B: Use STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY to load each array element as a separate row
choices.C: JSON arrays load as a single row and cannot be split
choices.D: JSON arrays must first be converted to CSV format

questions.explanation: The STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY file format option removes the outer array brackets so each element becomes its own row in the VARIANT column, rather than loading the entire array as one row.

questions.question: Which approach is appropriate when files arrive irregularly and you want hands-off, near-real-time loading without managing a warehouse?

choices.A: Scheduled bulk COPY on a large warehouse
choices.B: Snowpipe auto-ingest (serverless)
choices.C: Manual PUT and COPY
choices.D: External tables only

questions.explanation: Snowpipe auto-ingest is serverless and event-driven, automatically loading files shortly after they arrive without you provisioning or managing a virtual warehouse—ideal for irregular, near-real-time ingestion.

questions.question: Which is the correct general direction of the PUT and GET commands?

choices.A: PUT downloads files, GET uploads files to a stage
choices.B: PUT uploads to a stage, GET downloads from a stage
choices.C: Both PUT and GET upload files to a stage or location
choices.D: Both PUT and GET download files from a stage or storage

questions.explanation: PUT uploads local files to an internal stage; GET downloads files from an internal stage to the local file system. Neither works on external stages.

questions.question: Which statement about PUT and external stages is correct?

choices.A: PUT can upload directly to external stages in cloud buckets without any limitations
choices.B: PUT only works with internal stages; for external stages you upload via the cloud provider's tools
choices.C: PUT works equally well with internal and external stages
choices.D: PUT is exclusively used for unloading to stages and cannot be used for uploading

questions.explanation: PUT (and GET) operate only on internal stages. To place files in an external stage's underlying bucket, you use the cloud provider's upload mechanisms (e.g., AWS CLI, Azure tools).

questions.question: Which statement about a CTE (Common Table Expression) is correct?

choices.A: It permanently stores results in a temporary table
choices.B: It defines a named, temporary result set within a single query using WITH
choices.C: It replaces the need for all view objects
choices.D: It requires a warehouse restart to execute

questions.explanation: A CTE, introduced with WITH, defines a named temporary result set usable within the scope of a single statement, improving readability and enabling recursion.

questions.question: Which is true about deterministic vs. non-deterministic functions and the result cache?

choices.A: Non-deterministic functions (e.g., CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, RANDOM) can prevent result cache reuse
choices.B: All functions support result caching equally without any determinism requirements
choices.C: Deterministic functions prevent result cache from functioning in any context
choices.D: Result caching behavior is independent of function determinism characteristics

questions.explanation: Queries using non-deterministic functions like RANDOM() or certain time functions may not be eligible for result cache reuse because results can change between executions.

questions.question: Which function returns the number of elements in an ARRAY?

choices.A: ARRAY_SIZE
choices.B: LENGTH
choices.C: COUNT
choices.D: CARDINALITY only for tables

questions.explanation: ARRAY_SIZE returns the number of elements in an ARRAY. LENGTH works on strings/binary, and COUNT is an aggregate over rows.

questions.question: Which approach correctly extracts a nested value and casts it to a number from a VARIANT column v?

choices.A: v:amount::NUMBER
choices.B: NUMBER(v.amount)
choices.C: CAST(v AS amount)
choices.D: v[amount]

questions.explanation: v:amount accesses the 'amount' path in the VARIANT, and ::NUMBER casts it to a numeric type. This colon path notation with a cast is the idiomatic way to extract typed values from semi-structured data.

questions.question: Which DDL command changes a table's clustering key?

choices.A: ALTER TABLE ... CLUSTER BY (...)
choices.B: CREATE INDEX
choices.C: ALTER WAREHOUSE
choices.D: REINDEX TABLE

questions.explanation: ALTER TABLE <name> CLUSTER BY (expr, ...) defines or changes a table's clustering key. Snowflake does not use traditional CREATE INDEX statements.

questions.question: Which is the correct behavior of a standard stream after the source table is fully consumed by a DML?

choices.A: The stream is automatically dropped by the system
choices.B: The stream's offset advances; querying it again shows no rows until new changes occur
choices.C: The stream continues displaying the same rows indefinitely
choices.D: The source table is truncated after consumption

questions.explanation: Once consumed within a committed DML transaction, the stream advances its offset; subsequent reads return no change rows until further DML modifies the source table.

questions.question: Which function returns the position of a substring within a string?

choices.A: POSITION or CHARINDEX
choices.B: SUBSTRING
choices.C: REPLACE
choices.D: TRIM

questions.explanation: POSITION(substring IN string) (or CHARINDEX) returns the 1-based index where the substring first appears, or 0 if not found.

questions.question: Which clause makes a MERGE handle rows present in the source but not the target?

choices.A: WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE
choices.B: WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT
choices.C: WHEN MATCHED THEN DELETE
choices.D: WHEN SOURCE EMPTY

questions.explanation: WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT inserts source rows that have no matching target row, the 'insert new records' part of an upsert.

questions.question: Which best describes the GROUP BY clause?

choices.A: It filters and removes individual rows from the result
choices.B: It groups rows sharing values in specified columns so aggregates compute per group
choices.C: It orders the result set in ascending or descending sequence
choices.D: It joins two tables using matching column values

questions.explanation: GROUP BY partitions rows into groups based on the listed expressions, allowing aggregate functions (SUM, COUNT, etc.) to be computed per group.

questions.question: Which is true about GROUP BY ALL in Snowflake?

choices.A: GROUP BY ALL groups each row individually
choices.B: It automatically groups by all non-aggregated columns in the SELECT list
choices.C: GROUP BY ALL disables all aggregation in the query
choices.D: GROUP BY ALL is invalid syntax in Snowflake

questions.explanation: GROUP BY ALL is a convenience that groups by all SELECT-list expressions that are not aggregate functions, reducing the need to list grouping columns explicitly.

questions.question: Which is the result type of PARSE_JSON('{"a":1}')?

choices.A: VARCHAR
choices.B: VARIANT (OBJECT)
choices.C: NUMBER
choices.D: BOOLEAN

questions.explanation: PARSE_JSON returns a VARIANT; for a JSON object input the underlying type is OBJECT, which can be navigated with path notation.

questions.question: Which is a correct way to convert a VARIANT array into rows with their index?

choices.A: Use LATERAL FLATTEN and reference the value and index columns
choices.B: PIVOT can extract array elements with their index positions
choices.C: GROUP BY can expand arrays into individual rows
choices.D: ARRAY_AGG can expand arrays with index information

questions.explanation: LATERAL FLATTEN(input => v) returns columns including VALUE and INDEX for each array element, letting you expand an array into rows with positional index.

questions.question: Which is true about views referencing other views?

choices.A: Views are prohibited from referencing other views
choices.B: Views can be layered (a view can select from other views)
choices.C: Only secure views can reference other view objects
choices.D: Views can only reference base tables or external stages

questions.explanation: Views can be built on top of other views, enabling layered abstractions. Each layer's query is resolved at execution time.

questions.question: Which statement about Snowflake's handling of NULLs in aggregate functions is correct?

choices.A: Aggregates include NULL values in all calculations
choices.B: Most aggregates (e.g., SUM, AVG, COUNT(col)) ignore NULL values
choices.C: Aggregate functions fail when any NULL value is present
choices.D: COUNT(*) excludes rows that contain any NULL value

questions.explanation: Aggregate functions like SUM, AVG, and COUNT(column) ignore NULLs. COUNT(*) counts all rows including those with NULLs. This is standard SQL behavior.

questions.question: Which is the correct way to alias a column in a SELECT?

choices.A: SELECT col new_name (using AS optionally)
choices.B: SELECT ALIAS(col)
choices.C: SELECT RENAME col
choices.D: SELECT col -> new_name

questions.explanation: You alias a column with 'col AS new_name' (or simply 'col new_name'). The AS keyword is optional in Snowflake.

questions.question: Which is true about a clone's independence from its source after creation?

choices.A: Changes to the clone affect the source table due to shared underlying storage
choices.B: The clone and source are independent; modifying one does not affect the other
choices.C: They remain permanently linked; modifications to source affect all clones
choices.D: The clone is read-only and cannot be modified after the initial clone operation

questions.explanation: After cloning, the clone is fully independent. Thanks to copy-on-write, changes to either the clone or source are isolated and do not affect the other.

questions.question: Which best explains why dropping and recreating a large table can affect Time Travel/Fail-safe storage costs?

choices.A: It has no storage impact at all, since dropping and recreating a table immediately discards every prior version cost-free
choices.B: The dropped version remains recoverable in Time Travel/Fail-safe, temporarily retaining storage for both old and new data
choices.C: It immediately frees all storage and permanently removes every historical version, so no Time Travel or Fail-safe data lingers
choices.D: It disables encryption on the original table while enabling encryption on the newly created table, changing only security

questions.explanation: After dropping, the old data persists through Time Travel and Fail-safe windows. If you also load new data, you temporarily pay storage for both, until the old version's retention/Fail-safe expires.

questions.question: Which is true about replicating shares vs. databases?

choices.A: Only databases can be replicated; shares are local and cannot be replicated
choices.B: Both databases and account objects can be part of replication/failover groups for cross-region availability
choices.C: Shares cannot be used after replication and must be recreated manually by administrators
choices.D: Replication is only for warehouses and compute infrastructure, not data objects

questions.explanation: Replication and failover groups can include databases and other account objects (roles, warehouses metadata, shares, etc.), enabling cross-region/cloud business continuity and consistent sharing setups.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a failover group (vs. a replication group)?

choices.A: Only one-way copy with no ability to failover to the secondary account
choices.B: To enable failover/failback of the replicated objects to a secondary account for disaster recovery
choices.C: To share data with consumers across regions and cloud platforms
choices.D: To mask or redact data in the replicated database for security purposes

questions.explanation: A failover group (Business Critical+) extends replication by allowing you to promote the secondary to primary (failover) and later fail back, supporting disaster recovery and business continuity.

questions.question: Which is true about encryption key management responsibilities by default (without Tri-Secret Secure)?

choices.A: The customer manages all keys
choices.B: Snowflake fully manages encryption keys automatically
choices.C: Keys are stored in plaintext
choices.D: Encryption must be enabled manually

questions.explanation: By default, Snowflake manages all encryption keys via its hierarchical key model with automatic rotation. Tri-Secret Secure (Business Critical) adds a customer-managed key into the hierarchy.

questions.question: With Tri-Secret Secure, what can a customer do that mitigates risk if their key is compromised or access must be revoked?

choices.A: Nothing; encryption keys are immutable and cannot be revoked or disabled
choices.B: Disable/revoke their customer-managed key, rendering data inaccessible (a 'kill switch')
choices.C: Delete Snowflake's portion of the composite key to revoke access
choices.D: Share the customer-managed key publicly to demonstrate security protocols

questions.explanation: Because the composite master key requires both the customer key and Snowflake's key, the customer can revoke their key to immediately render data unreadable—acting as a control/kill switch.

questions.question: Which is a valid use of Time Travel for data recovery?

choices.A: Recovering data from 10 years in the past for historical analysis and reporting
choices.B: Restoring a table to its state before an accidental UPDATE that happened within the retention window
choices.C: Accessing Fail-safe data directly without contacting Snowflake support for recovery
choices.D: Querying another account's data through Time Travel queries without permissions

questions.explanation: Time Travel can recover from accidental DML (e.g., a bad UPDATE/DELETE) by querying or cloning the table as it existed before the change, as long as it's within the retention period.

questions.question: Which is the correct precedence: when does Fail-safe begin for a permanent table?

choices.A: Immediately when data is created
choices.B: After the Time Travel retention period ends
choices.C: Only when the account is deleted
choices.D: Never for permanent tables

questions.explanation: Fail-safe begins only after the Time Travel retention period elapses, providing an additional 7 days during which only Snowflake can recover the data.

questions.question: Which is true about the consumer's ability to re-share data they received via a share?

choices.A: Consumers can freely re-share inbound shared data to any third-party account
choices.B: By default, consumers cannot re-share inbound shared data to other accounts
choices.C: Re-sharing is automatic and immediate for all inbound shared data
choices.D: Consumers must re-share the data within 24 hours of receiving the share

questions.explanation: Inbound shared data generally cannot be re-shared by the consumer to other accounts; sharing is controlled by the original provider, preserving data governance.

questions.question: Which feature enables monetizing a dataset to many consumers with usage-based pricing while keeping a single copy?

choices.A: Reader accounts only, which allow free access to datasets
choices.B: Snowflake Marketplace paid listings backed by Secure Data Sharing
choices.C: Manual file exports that create data copies for each consumer
choices.D: Replication groups that distribute data across multiple geographic regions

questions.explanation: Paid Marketplace listings let providers monetize data products via Secure Data Sharing—consumers access a single live copy, and providers can set pricing terms without duplicating data per consumer.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which statement about a virtual warehouse and the data it can access is correct?

choices.A: Each warehouse has exclusive access to its own private partitioned data
choices.B: Any warehouse can access any data in the account it has privileges for, since storage is shared
choices.C: Warehouses physically store and maintain the data they execute queries against
choices.D: Raw data must be copied into each warehouse before it can be queried

questions.explanation: Because storage is centralized and shared, any warehouse can query any data the user/role is authorized to access. Warehouses are stateless compute; they don't own the data.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is the best description of how Snowflake achieves elasticity?

choices.A: By requiring manual hardware provisioning and capacity planning
choices.B: By instantly resizing warehouses and adding clusters on demand, with per-second billing
choices.C: By limiting each account to use a single static-sized warehouse configuration
choices.D: By caching and storing all data locally on warehouse compute nodes

questions.explanation: Snowflake's elasticity comes from on-demand compute: warehouses can be resized in seconds and multi-cluster warehouses auto-scale out/in, billed per-second, matching capacity to workload.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is a serverless feature in Snowflake?

choices.A: A user-configured and user-managed XL warehouse with continuous execution
choices.B: Snowpipe, Automatic Clustering, Materialized View maintenance, and serverless Tasks
choices.C: Manual COPY commands that explicitly load data into internal stages
choices.D: The SnowSQL command-line interface for interactive query execution

questions.explanation: Serverless features (Snowpipe, Automatic Clustering, MV maintenance, serverless tasks, search optimization) use Snowflake-managed compute that scales automatically without a user-managed warehouse.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: What advantage does columnar storage give analytical queries?

choices.A: Faster execution of single-row insertion and transactional updates
choices.B: Efficient scanning and compression of only the needed columns for analytical aggregations
choices.C: Better performance for transactional point updates and OLTP operations
choices.D: It eliminates the requirement for virtual compute resources entirely

questions.explanation: Columnar storage lets analytical queries read only the columns they need and benefit from high compression on similar values per column, which is ideal for scans and aggregations over large datasets.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which best describes Snowflake's pricing for cloud services compute?

choices.A: Cloud Services compute is always billed at the standard full rate
choices.B: Free up to 10% of daily compute credits; only the excess is billed
choices.C: Cloud Services compute charges are based on the per-user licensing model
choices.D: Cloud Services compute is never billed to any Snowflake accounts

questions.explanation: Cloud Services usage is billed only for the portion exceeding 10% of the day's warehouse compute credits, so typical balanced workloads incur little or no cloud services charge.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which workload would benefit most from a Snowpark-optimized warehouse?

choices.A: Simple and straightforward SELECT queries with minimal logic
choices.B: Memory-intensive ML training or large UDF/stored procedure workloads
choices.C: Small interactive dashboard and analytics queries with low complexity
choices.D: Tiny point-lookup queries returning single rows of data

questions.explanation: Snowpark-optimized warehouses provide more memory per node, benefiting memory-intensive workloads like ML model training and large Snowpark/UDF processing that would otherwise spill or fail on standard warehouses.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is true about Snowflake's multi-cloud support for a single account?

choices.A: A single Snowflake account can span several clouds and regions simultaneously, with no limit on where its data resides
choices.B: Each account resides in one cloud/region, but organizations can have accounts across clouds and replicate between them
choices.C: Snowflake accounts cannot use or reach any cloud infrastructure or external services, operating only in full isolation
choices.D: Only the AWS cloud provider is supported for any Snowflake account, so Azure and Google Cloud cannot host an account

questions.explanation: A given account is hosted in a single cloud region. To operate across clouds/regions, you create multiple accounts within an organization and use replication or sharing to move/expose data.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which best describes the relationship between credits and dollars in Snowflake?

choices.A: Credits are provided to every account at no charge and carry no associated dollar cost on the monthly invoice
choices.B: Credits are the unit of compute consumption; their dollar cost depends on edition, cloud, region, and contract
choices.C: One credit always equals exactly one US dollar everywhere, regardless of edition, cloud provider, region, or contract
choices.D: Credits are the unit used to measure and bill for data storage volume rather than for any compute consumption

questions.explanation: Credits measure compute usage. The monetary price per credit varies by Snowflake edition, cloud provider, region, and the customer's pricing/contract.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which object would you query to find historical credit usage across the whole organization?

choices.A: ORGANIZATION_USAGE schema in the SNOWFLAKE database
choices.B: The INFORMATION_SCHEMA schema contains all historical credit data
choices.C: The PUBLIC schema holds organization-wide usage and billing metrics
choices.D: User-created stages store detailed credit consumption records

questions.explanation: The ORGANIZATION_USAGE schema (accessible to ORGADMIN) provides organization-wide usage and cost data across all accounts, complementing per-account ACCOUNT_USAGE views.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which scenario best demonstrates workload isolation in Snowflake?

choices.A: All teams share a single warehouse and compete for compute resources equally
choices.B: Each team uses its own warehouse on the same shared data, so one team's heavy job doesn't slow others
choices.C: Each team duplicates and copies data into separate databases for isolation
choices.D: Only a single query can be executed globally across all teams at any time

questions.explanation: Workload isolation is achieved by giving each team/workload its own warehouse over the same shared storage, preventing resource contention while avoiding data duplication.

questions.question: Which is true about applying a network policy at the user level vs. account level?

choices.A: Only account-level network policies exist; user-level policy overrides are not supported
choices.B: A user-level network policy overrides the account-level policy for that user
choices.C: User-level network policies are silently ignored by Snowflake's access control system
choices.D: Account-level and user-level network policies cannot simultaneously exist in the same account

questions.explanation: Network policies can be set at the account level and overridden for specific users via a user-level policy, allowing tailored IP restrictions for particular users or service accounts.

questions.question: Which is a key reason to use External OAuth over Snowflake OAuth?

choices.A: External OAuth completely avoids using any identity provider or authorization server
choices.B: To integrate with an existing enterprise authorization server (e.g., Okta, Azure AD) issuing tokens for clients/BI tools
choices.C: External OAuth automatically disables RBAC and replaces it with attribute-based control
choices.D: External OAuth is the only mechanism supporting users who authenticate with passwords

questions.explanation: External OAuth lets clients and BI tools authenticate using tokens from an external authorization server you already use (Okta, Azure AD, Ping, etc.), integrating Snowflake into existing identity infrastructure.

questions.question: Which best describes least privilege in Snowflake RBAC?

choices.A: Grant the ACCOUNTADMIN role to all organization members for operational simplicity
choices.B: Grant each role only the privileges it needs to perform its function
choices.C: Avoid using roles entirely and implement alternative access control mechanisms
choices.D: Grant all possible privileges to the PUBLIC role for universal user access

questions.explanation: Least privilege means each role receives only the minimum privileges necessary for its purpose, reducing risk. This is implemented through carefully scoped roles and grants.

questions.question: Which is true about ownership transfer using GRANT OWNERSHIP?

choices.A: It creates a complete duplicate copy of the object with the new designated owner
choices.B: It changes the owning role; optionally REVOKE/COPY CURRENT GRANTS controls whether existing grants are preserved
choices.C: It automatically deletes all existing grant permissions and privilege assignments on the object
choices.D: It transfers ownership and requires the PUBLIC role to be granted for the operation to complete

questions.explanation: GRANT OWNERSHIP transfers the owning role of an object. You can specify COPY CURRENT GRANTS to retain existing privileges or REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS to clear them during transfer.

questions.question: Which is required for a user to log in using key pair authentication?

choices.A: A password set exclusively at the account level with no user-level password configuration
choices.B: The user's RSA public key registered in Snowflake and the matching private key on the client
choices.C: An external identity provider that issues and manages user authentication credentials
choices.D: The ACCOUNTADMIN role, which is automatically granted to all key pair authenticated users

questions.explanation: Key pair auth requires assigning the user's RSA public key in Snowflake (ALTER USER ... SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY) and the client signing with the corresponding private key.

questions.question: Why does Snowflake support two RSA public keys per user (RSA_PUBLIC_KEY and RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2)?

choices.A: For two different accounts
choices.B: To enable seamless key rotation without downtime
choices.C: To double the security strength
choices.D: One is for MFA

questions.explanation: Supporting two public keys allows rotating keys with zero downtime: you add the new key as the second, switch clients over, then remove the old key—avoiding an authentication outage.

questions.question: Which is true about the relationship between SYSADMIN and custom roles in best practice?

choices.A: Custom roles should never be granted to SYSADMIN because it creates circular hierarchy conflicts
choices.B: Custom roles should be granted to SYSADMIN so it can manage their objects
choices.C: SYSADMIN cannot own objects created by custom roles and has no access to them
choices.D: Custom roles must be granted directly to PUBLIC to be accessible by any user

questions.explanation: Granting custom roles to SYSADMIN (directly or via a hierarchy) lets SYSADMIN manage all objects those roles create, maintaining centralized administrative control.

questions.question: Which scenario best uses a row access policy?

choices.A: To mask and transform sensitive column values based on the querying user's role
choices.B: Restricting a regional manager to see only their region's rows in a shared sales table
choices.C: To limit warehouse size and scaling capabilities for queries
choices.D: To encrypt data at rest using customer-managed or Snowflake-managed encryption keys

questions.explanation: Row access policies implement row-level security, e.g., filtering a sales table so each manager sees only rows for their region, based on their role or a mapping table evaluated at query time.

questions.question: Which is true about combining a masking policy and a row access policy on the same table?

choices.A: Masking policies and row access policies cannot be simultaneously applied to the same table
choices.B: They can be used together—row access policy controls which rows are visible, masking controls how column values appear
choices.C: A masking policy takes absolute precedence over row access policies, overriding row filtering
choices.D: Only one policy per table is permitted; administrators must choose between row or column governance

questions.explanation: A table can have both a row access policy (row-level filtering) and column masking policies (column-level transformation) simultaneously, providing layered, fine-grained data governance.

questions.question: Which is a benefit of tag-based policies for governance at scale?

choices.A: They require manually applying policies to each new column as soon as it is created
choices.B: Policies attached to tags automatically protect any column assigned that tag, scaling governance
choices.C: Tag-based policies completely disable Role-Based Access Control across the account
choices.D: Tag-based policies only function on warehouse objects and cannot protect database or table objects

questions.explanation: By attaching a masking policy to a tag, every column tagged (now or in the future) inherits protection automatically, greatly simplifying consistent governance across many objects.

questions.question: Which is the recommended starting warehouse size for most workloads before tuning?

choices.A: Always provision 6XL warehouse regardless of actual requirements
choices.B: Start small (e.g., XS/S), measure, and scale up/out based on observed performance and queuing
choices.C: Always provision 4XL warehouse regardless of workload characteristics
choices.D: Warehouse size selection has negligible impact on performance

questions.explanation: A common practice is to start with a smaller warehouse, observe performance/queuing via the Query Profile and monitoring, then scale up (for slow complex queries) or out (for concurrency) as needed.

questions.question: Which is true about the impact of clustering on DML-heavy tables?

choices.A: Clustering incurs zero maintenance or operational costs of any kind
choices.B: Frequent DML can increase automatic clustering (reclustering) credit costs, so weigh benefits vs. cost
choices.C: Clustering prevents all DML operations on the table indefinitely
choices.D: Clustering optimizations eliminate all storage requirements for the table

questions.explanation: Automatic Clustering consumes serverless credits to maintain clustering as data changes. On highly volatile tables, reclustering cost can be significant, so evaluate whether the query benefit justifies it.

questions.question: Which is the best way to reduce data scanned for queries filtering by date on a huge events table?

choices.A: Disable the result cache to improve date filter efficiency
choices.B: Cluster by (or naturally load ordered by) the date column to improve pruning
choices.C: Increase AUTO_SUSPEND to retain clustering metadata
choices.D: Use a smaller warehouse configuration for date filtering

questions.explanation: Organizing data by the date column (via load order or a clustering key) lets Snowflake prune partitions outside the queried date range, dramatically reducing bytes scanned.

questions.question: Which monitoring approach best supports chargeback by department?

choices.A: Operate a single shared warehouse for all department queries without separation
choices.B: Per-department warehouses and querying WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY (and tags) to attribute credits
choices.C: Disable resource monitors for improved cost transparency and reduced overhead
choices.D: Rely exclusively on the result cache for cost tracking and chargebacks

questions.explanation: Assigning warehouses per department and analyzing WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY (optionally with object tagging) lets you attribute credit consumption to each department for chargeback/showback.

questions.question: Which is a sign that a query is well optimized in the Query Profile?

choices.A: Scanning most partitions with significant remote spilling and data movement
choices.B: High pruning (few partitions scanned), minimal spilling, and time dominated by necessary processing
choices.C: Extended query queuing times in the execution queue before processing
choices.D: Large volumes of data spilled to remote storage indicating performance issues

questions.explanation: A well-optimized query prunes effectively (scans few partitions), avoids spilling, and spends time on essential work rather than queuing or I/O overflow.

questions.question: Which action helps when a multi-cluster warehouse rarely scales beyond one cluster yet single queries are slow?

choices.A: Increase the MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT parameter for the warehouse
choices.B: Scale up the warehouse size (more compute per cluster) rather than adding clusters
choices.C: Lower the AUTO_SUSPEND value to reduce cold cache periods
choices.D: Disable the result cache for individual query execution

questions.explanation: If concurrency is low (rarely multiple clusters) but individual queries are slow, the bottleneck is per-query compute—scaling up the warehouse size helps more than adding clusters (which addresses concurrency).

questions.question: Which is true about the cost of storing Time Travel and Fail-safe data?

choices.A: Time Travel and Fail-safe storage are provided at no cost
choices.B: Historical (Time Travel) and Fail-safe data consume storage that is billed
