choices.C: Only Fail-safe data retention is subject to billing
choices.D: Only Time Travel data retention incurs storage charges

questions.explanation: Both Time Travel and Fail-safe retain historical data versions that occupy storage and are billed. Choosing transient tables or shorter retention reduces this storage cost where appropriate.

questions.question: Which is the best first action when a dashboard is slow due to many users hitting it at 9am?

choices.A: Increase the warehouse size to 6XL for peak load hours to handle concurrency
choices.B: Enable multi-cluster (scale out) so additional clusters absorb the concurrency spike
choices.C: Disable the AUTO_RESUME feature temporarily to prevent warehouse activation
choices.D: Reduce the Time Travel retention period settings to improve query performance

questions.explanation: A morning concurrency spike is best handled by scaling out with a multi-cluster warehouse that adds clusters during peak demand and removes them afterward, preventing queuing without permanently oversizing.

questions.question: Which is a reason to consolidate many tiny warehouses into fewer right-sized ones?

choices.A: To increase the amount of idle time and reduce active usage patterns
choices.B: To improve cache reuse and reduce repeated 60-second minimum charges from frequent cold starts
choices.C: To disable the auto-scaling feature entirely across all warehouse tiers
choices.D: To increase the total storage allocation for all tables in the account

questions.explanation: Too many sporadically used warehouses cause frequent cold starts (60-second minimums) and poor cache reuse. Consolidating workloads onto fewer right-sized warehouses can improve efficiency and reduce cost.

questions.question: Which best balances performance and cost for a nightly batch that processes huge joins once per day?

choices.A: A small warehouse left running continuously all day and night, so the nightly batch always has compute immediately available
choices.B: A larger warehouse that runs only during the batch (auto-suspend after), finishing faster at similar total credits
choices.C: A multi-cluster warehouse configured with a high minimum cluster count, guaranteeing reserved capacity for the single batch
choices.D: Disabling auto-suspend and keeping a 6XL warehouse running around the clock so the batch never waits for a resume

questions.explanation: For a heavy, parallelizable nightly job, a larger warehouse that runs briefly (then auto-suspends) often completes much faster at comparable total credits, and avoids idle cost the rest of the day.

questions.question: Which is true about the default compression for unloaded files?

choices.A: Files are never compressed when unloaded regardless of settings
choices.B: By default, unloaded files are gzip-compressed unless you specify otherwise
choices.C: Only Parquet and JSON formats support compression on unload
choices.D: File compression is not supported for any unload operation

questions.explanation: COPY INTO <location> compresses output files with gzip by default. You can change or disable compression via the file format's COMPRESSION option.

questions.question: Which is the correct way to load only specific columns from a CSV into a table with different column order?

choices.A: COPY INTO table FROM (SELECT $2, $1 FROM @stage) — reorder using positional references
choices.B: Loading specific columns in different order from CSV is impossible
choices.C: Using PUT with column mapping to reorder fields during load
choices.D: Renaming the table after load to match CSV column order

questions.explanation: In a COPY transformation, positional references ($1, $2, ...) let you select and reorder columns from a CSV to match the target table's column order or pick a subset.

questions.question: Which best describes the purpose of the SKIP_BYTE_ORDER_MARK option?

choices.A: It skips the header row so it is not loaded as data
choices.B: It skips the BOM at the start of a UTF-encoded file so it isn't loaded as data
choices.C: It compresses the file before loading to the stage
choices.D: It removes column delimiters to consolidate fields

questions.explanation: SKIP_BYTE_ORDER_MARK (default TRUE) skips a byte order mark at the start of a file so the BOM characters are not mistakenly loaded into the first column.

questions.question: Which scenario warrants using ENFORCE_LENGTH = TRUE (the default) during load?

choices.A: When you want oversize strings to error out rather than be silently truncated
choices.B: ENFORCE_LENGTH = TRUE always truncates strings exceeding limits
choices.C: ENFORCE_LENGTH = TRUE only affects numeric columns, not strings
choices.D: ENFORCE_LENGTH = TRUE is used to skip header rows

questions.explanation: ENFORCE_LENGTH = TRUE causes the load to error if a string exceeds the target column length, protecting against silent data loss. Setting TRUNCATECOLUMNS = TRUE (ENFORCE_LENGTH = FALSE) truncates instead.

questions.question: Which is required before COPY can read from a named external stage pointing to S3?

choices.A: The stage must reference a valid location and have access (credentials or storage integration)
choices.B: No authorization is required; Snowflake can read any bucket
choices.C: Access requires creating a Snowflake reader account in same region
choices.D: Time Travel must be enabled on the bucket for COPY reads

questions.explanation: The external stage must point to the correct cloud path and be authorized to access it—typically via a storage integration (preferred) or credentials—so COPY can read the files.

questions.question: Which is true about loading large numbers of very small files with Snowpipe?

choices.A: Loading many tiny files through Snowpipe is always the most efficient and cost-effective ingestion pattern available
choices.B: Per-file overhead can make many tiny files less cost-efficient; batching into larger files is often better
choices.C: Snowpipe is unable to load or process any file that is smaller than one megabyte, so tiny files must be padded first
choices.D: Small files carry no per-file processing overhead in Snowpipe, so the number of files has no effect on ingestion cost

questions.explanation: Snowpipe charges a small per-file overhead. Loading huge numbers of tiny files increases overhead cost; consolidating into appropriately sized files (where possible) improves efficiency.

questions.question: Which command would you use to remove files from an internal stage?

choices.A: REMOVE @stage/path
choices.B: DROP FILE
choices.C: DELETE STAGE
choices.D: PURGE STAGE

questions.explanation: REMOVE @stage (RM) deletes files from an internal stage. PURGE = TRUE on COPY can also delete files automatically after a successful load.

questions.question: Which approach best handles schema drift when ingesting evolving Parquet files?

choices.A: Hardcode all columns and fail immediately on schema changes in the data
choices.B: Use INFER_SCHEMA / schema evolution features and MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME to adapt to changing columns
choices.C: Convert all Parquet files to CSV before loading to avoid drift
choices.D: Disable file format definitions to allow automatic adaptation to schema changes

questions.explanation: INFER_SCHEMA, table schema evolution, and MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME help accommodate changing/added columns in evolving Parquet files, reducing breakage from schema drift.

questions.question: Which is true about the relationship between a stage and a file format?

choices.A: A stage cannot be associated with or use a file format
choices.B: A file format can be associated with a stage or specified in the COPY command
choices.C: File formats are only relevant for unloading operations
choices.D: A stage must always define its own storage integration

questions.explanation: You can attach a default file format to a stage or pass a file format (named or inline) in the COPY statement. COPY-level settings override the stage's default.

questions.question: Which is the most reliable way to verify exactly which rows failed during a large load?

choices.A: Estimate which rows failed by comparing row counts and total file sizes
choices.B: Use VALIDATION_MODE or query COPY_HISTORY / validate function to inspect rejected records
choices.C: Re-run the load with FORCE = TRUE to check error output
choices.D: Delete the target table and reload from backup to identify error rows

questions.explanation: VALIDATION_MODE (e.g., RETURN_ALL_ERRORS) and the COPY_HISTORY view / VALIDATE function let you identify the specific rejected rows and reasons, enabling targeted remediation.

questions.question: Which is true about a UDF's ability to perform DML (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE)?

choices.A: UDFs can perform INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations
choices.B: UDFs cannot perform DML or DDL; use stored procedures for that
choices.C: Only SQL UDFs have the capability to perform DML operations
choices.D: UDFs can drop tables and other database objects

questions.explanation: UDFs are restricted to returning values and cannot perform DML/DDL or transaction control. Stored procedures are used when you need to execute DML/DDL and procedural logic.

questions.question: Which is correct about a stored procedure's return value?

choices.A: A stored procedure cannot return any value to its caller and is limited to performing side effects within the current session
choices.B: It can return a scalar value or a tabular result (e.g., via RESULTSET / table return), depending on language
choices.C: A stored procedure always returns a tabular result set, regardless of the language or the logic that it was written in
choices.D: A stored procedure can only return a boolean TRUE or FALSE that indicates whether its internal execution succeeded

questions.explanation: Stored procedures can return a scalar value, and (depending on language/features like RESULTSET or TABLE return types) can also return tabular results. They support richer logic than UDFs.

questions.question: Which is the correct way to flatten a JSON array stored in column data of table t?

choices.A: SELECT f.value FROM t, LATERAL FLATTEN(input => t.data) f
choices.B: SELECT FLATTEN(t.data) without LATERAL for each row
choices.C: SELECT t.data.* for direct nested property access
choices.D: SELECT EXPLODE(t.data) for element expansion

questions.explanation: The idiomatic pattern is a LATERAL FLATTEN: 'FROM t, LATERAL FLATTEN(input => t.data) f', then reference f.value (and f.index) for each element.

questions.question: Which is true about Snowflake's transactions?

choices.A: Snowflake does not support ACID transaction semantics in any way
choices.B: Snowflake supports ACID transactions; statements can be grouped with BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK
choices.C: Only single-statement transactions are supported
choices.D: Transactions can span multiple Snowflake accounts across regions

questions.explanation: Snowflake provides ACID-compliant transactions. You can group multiple statements explicitly with BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK; otherwise each statement is its own auto-committed transaction.

questions.question: Which function returns the current session's active role?

choices.A: CURRENT_ROLE()
choices.B: CURRENT_USER()
choices.C: CURRENT_WAREHOUSE()
choices.D: CURRENT_SESSION()

questions.explanation: CURRENT_ROLE() returns the name of the role currently active in the session, commonly used inside masking and row access policies.

questions.question: Which is correct about how Snowflake evaluates a masking policy referencing CURRENT_ROLE()?

choices.A: The policy is evaluated once when the table is created and cached permanently
choices.B: It evaluates at query time for each query, returning masked or clear values based on the active role
choices.C: The policy permanently modifies stored data values
choices.D: The policy ignores the role and applies uniformly to all users without variation

questions.explanation: Masking policies are evaluated at query time. The policy logic checks the querying context (e.g., CURRENT_ROLE()) each time, returning clear or masked data accordingly without altering stored values.

questions.question: Which is true about CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE?

choices.A: It preserves the old table's data and appends new rows to the table
choices.B: It drops the existing table and creates a new one, resetting data and starting fresh Time Travel
choices.C: It only adds new columns without affecting existing data
choices.D: It renames the table while keeping the same data and structure

questions.explanation: CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE drops the existing table (its data goes to Time Travel/Fail-safe) and creates a new table. The new table has its own fresh Time Travel timeline.

questions.question: Which is a key consideration when using CREATE OR REPLACE on a table that others depend on?

choices.A: It has no effect whatsoever on dependent objects or their privileges, because Snowflake transparently rebinds every reference for you
choices.B: It can break dependent objects/grants since it's a drop+create; CREATE OR ALTER or careful change management may be safer
choices.C: It automatically preserves and reapplies all grants on the object and its dependents, so no privilege is ever lost in the process
choices.D: It cannot be executed against a table or view that other objects depend on, so existing dependencies make the statement fail safely

questions.explanation: Because CREATE OR REPLACE drops and recreates the object, dependent views/grants can be affected. For safer in-place changes, consider ALTER TABLE or CREATE OR ALTER where applicable, and manage dependencies.

questions.question: Which is true about the COPY GRANTS option on CREATE OR REPLACE?

choices.A: It deletes all existing privileges on the object
choices.B: It preserves the existing privileges (grants) on the recreated object
choices.C: It copies only table data during recreation
choices.D: COPY GRANTS is invalid syntax

questions.explanation: Adding COPY GRANTS to CREATE OR REPLACE retains the privileges that existed on the previous object, avoiding the need to re-grant after recreating it.

questions.question: Which is the correct behavior of a stream created on a view?

choices.A: Streams cannot be created on any view object, including standard views and materialized views, only on base tables
choices.B: A stream can be created on certain views (e.g., over a single table or supported constructs) to track changes
choices.C: Streams function exclusively on materialized views, since only their persisted results expose the change metadata needed
choices.D: Streams operate only on permanent tables and never on views, external tables, or other derived constructs in Snowflake

questions.explanation: Snowflake supports streams on views (with certain limitations), allowing CDC tracking through a view definition, in addition to streams on tables and directory tables.

questions.question: Which is the maximum number of accounts a provider can add to a single share?

choices.A: Exactly one consumer account can be granted access to a single share
choices.B: Multiple consumer accounts can be added to the same share
choices.C: Shares cannot include accounts; they only reference database schemas
choices.D: Only reader accounts can be added to a share

questions.explanation: A single share can be granted to multiple consumer accounts, so many consumers can access the same shared objects from one share definition.

questions.question: Which is true about querying shared data and Time Travel?

choices.A: Consumers can freely Time Travel into the provider's full history, querying any earlier version within the retention window
choices.B: Consumers have read-only access to current shared data and generally cannot use Time Travel on the provider's objects
choices.C: Consumers directly control and configure the provider's Time Travel retention settings on the objects shared with them
choices.D: Time Travel operates bidirectionally across the share, so the provider and consumer each see the other's historical versions

questions.explanation: Consumers access live, read-only shared data. They do not manage the provider's Time Travel and cannot perform Time Travel queries on the provider's shared objects as if they owned them.

questions.question: Which best describes a benefit of cloning for backup/testing within an account?

choices.A: It copies data to another cloud provider for geographic redundancy
choices.B: It instantly creates an independent, low-cost copy for testing without duplicating storage initially
choices.C: It encrypts the data again with consumer-managed keys and algorithms
choices.D: It shares data externally with third-party accounts and organizations

questions.explanation: Zero-copy cloning quickly produces an independent copy for dev/test or snapshots, with no upfront storage duplication—ideal for safe experimentation within the same account.

questions.question: Which is true about cross-region replication latency and consistency?

choices.A: It is always instantaneous and synchronous with no latency between regions
choices.B: Replication is asynchronous; the secondary reflects the primary as of the last successful refresh
choices.C: It is synchronous and blocks all writes during the replication refresh
choices.D: Secondary is always ahead of the primary with leading-edge data

questions.explanation: Database/account replication is asynchronous. The secondary is updated by periodic refreshes and reflects the primary's state as of the last completed refresh, so there is some lag.

questions.question: Which is true about Fail-safe configurability?

choices.A: Fail-safe can be configured from 0 to 90 days based on account settings
choices.B: Fail-safe is a fixed, non-configurable 7-day period for permanent objects
choices.C: Fail-safe is 1 day by default for all object types in all editions
choices.D: Fail-safe can be disabled on permanent tables through ALTER TABLE

questions.explanation: Fail-safe for permanent objects is a fixed 7 days and cannot be configured or disabled. Only Time Travel retention is configurable. Transient/temporary objects have no Fail-safe.

questions.question: Which approach lets you recover a specific table that was accidentally dropped 2 hours ago (retention = 1 day)?

choices.A: Contact Snowflake support to recover the table from Fail-safe storage
choices.B: UNDROP TABLE, since it is within the Time Travel retention period
choices.C: Recreate it manually from scratch using original DDL scripts
choices.D: It is unrecoverable after the table has been dropped

questions.explanation: With a 1-day retention, a table dropped 2 hours ago is still within Time Travel, so UNDROP TABLE restores it instantly—no need to involve Fail-safe (which is only after retention expires).

questions.question: Which is true about secure views and data sharing?

choices.A: Non-secure views are recommended for sharing due to better performance
choices.B: Secure views are recommended when sharing because they hide definitions and protect underlying data
choices.C: Views cannot be shared through Secure Data Sharing mechanisms
choices.D: Only tables can be shared; views are excluded from sharing mechanisms

questions.explanation: When sharing, secure views are recommended because they conceal the view's logic and prevent optimizations that could leak underlying data, supporting safe, governed sharing.

questions.question: Which is the best way for a provider to give different consumers access to different subsets of a table?

choices.A: Create one comprehensive share with full access to the table for all consumers
choices.B: Use secure views/row access policies keyed on consumer identity, or separate shares per consumer subset
choices.C: Email CSV extracts to each consumer with their specific data subset
choices.D: Grant ACCOUNTADMIN privilege to consumers to manage their own access

questions.explanation: Providers can tailor access using secure views or row access policies that filter by consumer identity (e.g., CURRENT_ACCOUNT) within a shared object, or by creating separate shares for different subsets.

questions.question: Which is true about the storage cost of shared data?

choices.A: Each consumer pays storage for a complete copy of the shared data
choices.B: The provider stores one copy; consumers pay no storage for shared data, only compute to query it
choices.C: Snowflake stores duplicate copies of data per consumer automatically
choices.D: Storage costs are doubled per share to maintain redundancy

questions.explanation: Secure Data Sharing keeps a single copy at the provider. Consumers incur no storage cost for the shared data—only their own compute when querying it.

questions.question: Which is the correct distinction between a private listing and a Marketplace (public) listing?

choices.A: Private listings are visible to every Snowflake customer browsing the public Marketplace, just like any publicly published listing
choices.B: Private listings are shared with specific consumers/accounts, while Marketplace listings are publicly discoverable
choices.C: Private listings and Marketplace listings are identical in functionality and visibility, reaching the same broad set of consumers
choices.D: Marketplace listings cannot be monetized or offered with any pricing, so all public data sets must be provided free of charge

questions.explanation: A private listing targets specific consumer accounts (not publicly visible), whereas a public Marketplace listing is discoverable by any Snowflake customer. Both can support cross-region auto-fulfillment and monetization.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which best describes how Snowflake handles concurrent read and write to the same table?

choices.A: Write operations completely block all concurrent read activity
choices.B: Snapshot isolation lets readers see a consistent version while writers create new micro-partitions
choices.C: Only a single user can access a table at any moment in time
choices.D: Read operations return partially committed and uncommitted write data

questions.explanation: Snowflake uses snapshot isolation backed by immutable micro-partitions: readers see a consistent snapshot while writers produce new partitions, avoiding read/write blocking and dirty reads.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which capability allows querying data without moving it, directly in external cloud storage?

choices.A: Internal stages are used for querying data in place
choices.B: External tables (and Iceberg tables) over external storage
choices.C: The result cache enables direct querying of external cloud storage
choices.D: Sequences provide mechanisms for external table access

questions.explanation: External tables (and Snowflake-managed/Apache Iceberg tables) let you query data in external cloud storage in place, supporting data lake architectures without ingesting the data into Snowflake's internal format.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is true about Iceberg tables in Snowflake?

choices.A: They exclusively use Snowflake's proprietary internal data format
choices.B: They use the open Apache Iceberg table format on customer-managed or Snowflake-managed storage
choices.C: Iceberg tables cannot be queried using standard SQL syntax
choices.D: They are functionally identical to temporary session-scoped tables

questions.explanation: Iceberg tables let Snowflake work with the open Apache Iceberg format, enabling interoperability with other engines while leveraging Snowflake's performance and governance over data in object storage.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is the main benefit of Snowflake's support for open table formats like Iceberg?

choices.A: It deepens vendor lock-in by storing the data in a proprietary Snowflake-only format that other engines cannot read
choices.B: Interoperability—data can be accessed by multiple engines while still governed and queried in Snowflake
choices.C: It disables SQL query capability over the open-format data, leaving it readable only by external batch processes
choices.D: It removes the need for any data storage infrastructure entirely, since open formats keep the data purely in memory

questions.explanation: Open formats like Iceberg promote interoperability, letting multiple tools/engines read the same data while Snowflake provides querying, performance, and governance—reducing lock-in.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is true about Snowflake's handling of structured table constraints (PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY)?

choices.A: Constraints are fully enforced like traditional OLTP database systems
choices.B: They are supported for metadata/informational purposes but generally not enforced (except NOT NULL)
choices.C: Constraints are not supported in Snowflake table definitions
choices.D: Constraints are enforced only on temporary session-scoped tables

questions.explanation: Snowflake allows defining PRIMARY KEY, UNIQUE, and FOREIGN KEY constraints for documentation and tool compatibility, but does not enforce them (NOT NULL is enforced). This is typical for analytical warehouses.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Why does Snowflake not enforce most constraints by default?

choices.A: Enforcing constraints would shrink available storage capacity and noticeably increase the monthly storage costs for large tables
choices.B: Enforcing them on massive analytical loads would be costly; analytics typically rely on upstream data quality
choices.C: The SQL language standard explicitly forbids enforcing constraints on columnar analytic tables, so Snowflake must comply
choices.D: Constraints have to be left unenforced because enforcing them would block SQL join operations between the related tables

questions.explanation: Enforcing uniqueness/referential integrity on large bulk loads is expensive and usually unnecessary for analytics, where data quality is managed upstream. Snowflake keeps constraints informational (except NOT NULL) for performance.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is a correct statement about Snowflake worksheets in Snowsight?

choices.A: They can only execute a single query one time in their lifetime
choices.B: They let you write and run SQL/Python, view results, and build charts interactively
choices.C: They exclusively store table row data and table definitions
choices.D: They completely replace virtual warehouses in Snowflake

questions.explanation: Snowsight worksheets provide an interactive environment to author and execute SQL (and Python via Snowpark), inspect results, and create visualizations and dashboards.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is true about how Snowflake bills for serverless features vs. user warehouses?

choices.A: Both serverless features and user-managed warehouses draw down the very same warehouse credits through one identical per-second metering mechanism
choices.B: Serverless features are billed at their own per-second serverless compute rates, separate from user-managed warehouse credits
choices.C: Serverless features are entirely free of charge and never appear as a separate consumption line item anywhere on the Snowflake bill
choices.D: User-managed virtual warehouses never consume any credits, so every compute charge on the account originates solely from serverless usage

questions.explanation: Serverless features (Snowpipe, automatic clustering, MV maintenance, etc.) consume Snowflake-managed compute billed at serverless rates, distinct from the credits consumed by user-managed virtual warehouses.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is the function of a directory table layered on a stage?

choices.A: To persist and store row-level table data in a stage
choices.B: To provide queryable metadata about files in the stage (path, size, last modified)
choices.C: To accelerate and optimize query execution speed and latency
choices.D: To mask and obfuscate sensitive column values for security

questions.explanation: A directory table exposes file metadata for a stage as a queryable table, useful for cataloging unstructured files and driving processing pipelines.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which best describes Snowflake Cortex Search or LLM functions' execution model?

choices.A: They execute exclusively on the client machine or user workstation
choices.B: They run as serverless, fully managed AI services invoked from SQL/Python within Snowflake
choices.C: They require manual API credentials to be passed with every function call
choices.D: They are unavailable for use within SQL query statements

questions.explanation: Snowflake Cortex provides serverless, managed AI/LLM and search capabilities callable directly from SQL or Python, keeping data and processing within Snowflake's governed environment.

questions.question: Which is true about the default role assigned to a newly created user with no DEFAULT_ROLE set?

choices.A: ACCOUNTADMIN
choices.B: PUBLIC (since every user has PUBLIC)
choices.C: SYSADMIN
choices.D: No role at all

questions.explanation: If no default role is specified and no other role is granted/active, the user operates under PUBLIC, which all users have. Administrators typically grant and set a more specific default role.

questions.question: Which is a correct statement about privilege checks for a query joining objects owned by different roles?

choices.A: Only the primary role's privileges matter; you may need secondary roles to access all objects
choices.B: Privileges are ignored for queries joining multiple objects owned by different roles
choices.C: Object ownership is irrelevant when executing queries; all privilege checks are bypassed
choices.D: Any query joining objects owned by different roles automatically fails immediately

questions.explanation: A query needs access to all referenced objects via the active role(s). If objects are owned/granted across different roles, enabling secondary roles (USE SECONDARY ROLES ALL) can let the session combine the needed privileges.

questions.question: Which is the recommended way to give a BI tool service account access with strong security?

choices.A: Share the human user's password with the BI tool to enable service account connectivity
choices.B: Create a dedicated service user with key pair or OAuth, least-privilege role, and a network policy
choices.C: Grant the BI service account the ACCOUNTADMIN role to provide all necessary permissions
choices.D: Use the PUBLIC role exclusively for all BI tool access without privilege limitations

questions.explanation: Best practice is a dedicated service account authenticated via key pair or OAuth, granted only the privileges it needs, and optionally restricted by a network policy—never sharing human credentials or over-privileging.

questions.question: Which is true about enforcing MFA for SSO users?

choices.A: Snowflake's built-in MFA always applies on top of SSO authentication for layered security
choices.B: When using federated SSO, MFA is typically enforced by the identity provider, not Snowflake's built-in MFA
choices.C: MFA cannot be used in conjunction with SSO; they are mutually exclusive authentication methods
choices.D: SSO completely disables all security measures and authentication verification requirements

questions.explanation: With federated SSO, authentication (including MFA) is handled by the external identity provider. Snowflake's built-in Duo MFA applies to users authenticating directly with Snowflake credentials.

questions.question: Which is true about the scope of a masking policy object?

choices.A: A masking policy is an account-level system object that is available across all databases
choices.B: It is a schema-level object created within a database/schema
choices.C: A masking policy is a warehouse-level configuration that controls warehouse access behavior
choices.D: A masking policy is a user profile attribute stored in the user account definition

questions.explanation: A masking policy is a schema-level object. It is created in a schema and then applied to columns, allowing centralized, reusable governance definitions.

questions.question: Which is the safest way to grant temporary elevated access for a one-time admin task?

choices.A: Permanently grant the ACCOUNTADMIN role to handle all elevated access requirements
choices.B: Grant a narrowly scoped role for the task, perform it, then revoke—following least privilege and auditing the action
choices.C: Disable Role-Based Access Control to bypass privilege restrictions during the task
choices.D: Share ACCOUNTADMIN credentials with authorized personnel to distribute elevated access

questions.explanation: Provision a least-privilege role scoped to the task, use it, then revoke it, while auditing via ACCESS/QUERY history. Avoid permanent over-privileging or credential sharing.

questions.question: Which view helps audit login attempts and detect failed authentications?

choices.A: ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY
choices.B: ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLES
choices.C: INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS
choices.D: SHOW WAREHOUSES

questions.explanation: LOGIN_HISTORY (in ACCOUNT_USAGE and INFORMATION_SCHEMA) records authentication attempts including success/failure, client info, and reasons—useful for security auditing and detecting anomalies.

questions.question: Which is the best response if you detect repeated failed logins from an unexpected IP for a service account?

choices.A: Ignore the failed login attempts as they present no meaningful security concern
choices.B: Investigate, tighten/apply a network policy, rotate credentials/keys, and review LOGIN_HISTORY/ACCESS_HISTORY
choices.C: Grant the service account additional privileges to prevent future authentication failures
choices.D: Disable encryption on the account to simplify authentication and prevent login failures

questions.explanation: A potential intrusion warrants investigation: restrict access via network policy, rotate keys/credentials, review audit views (LOGIN_HISTORY/ACCESS_HISTORY), and apply least privilege—standard incident response.

questions.question: Which is true about who can create a network policy and apply it account-wide?

choices.A: Any user with a standard role can create and apply network policies account-wide
choices.B: A role with the appropriate privileges (e.g., SECURITYADMIN/ACCOUNTADMIN) manages account-level network policies
choices.C: Only users with the PUBLIC role can create and manage account-level network policies
choices.D: Network policies cannot be created or applied in any Snowflake account configuration

questions.explanation: Creating and applying network policies (especially account-level) requires sufficiently privileged roles such as SECURITYADMIN or ACCOUNTADMIN, in line with security administration responsibilities.

questions.question: Which is the correct relationship between a tag and the objects it can be applied to?

choices.A: Tags apply only to warehouse objects for cost tracking and cannot be assigned to databases, schemas, tables, or individual columns
choices.B: Tags can be applied to many object types (databases, schemas, tables, columns, warehouses) for classification and cost/governance
choices.C: Tags can be assigned only to role objects for access-control purposes and are never permitted on data objects such as tables or columns
choices.D: Tags cannot be queried or retrieved through any view or function, because they exist purely as internal metadata hidden from all users

questions.explanation: Object tags are flexible labels that can be assigned to a wide range of objects (including columns and warehouses) to support data classification, cost attribution, and tag-based policies.

questions.question: Which is true about how warehouse size affects loading performance with COPY?

choices.A: Larger warehouse configurations always accelerate single file loads
choices.B: Larger warehouses can load more files in parallel, but a single file is limited to one thread
choices.C: Warehouse size has negligible effect on COPY performance
choices.D: Smaller warehouse sizes enable greater parallelization

questions.explanation: More compute means more threads to load many files concurrently. However, a single file is processed by one thread, so splitting data into multiple files is key to leveraging a larger warehouse for loads.

questions.question: Which is the best optimization when a frequently joined dimension table is repeatedly scanned in full?

choices.A: Apply clustering keys to the small dimension table so that its micro-partitions become more selective during the join
choices.B: Rely on caching and ensure the warehouse stays warm; small tables benefit from cache, not clustering
choices.C: Enable Search Optimization on every column of the dimension table to accelerate the repeated full scans during joins
choices.D: Replicate the dimension table out to other accounts so that each one can reuse its own cached copy more effectively

questions.explanation: Small dimension tables don't benefit from clustering; they're inexpensive to scan and are cached. Keeping the warehouse warm leverages the SSD/result cache for repeated joins.

questions.question: Which is a benefit of using QUERY_HISTORY for optimization?

choices.A: QUERY_HISTORY automatically encrypts sensitive query data before storage
choices.B: It identifies long-running, frequently-run, or heavy-scan queries to target for tuning
choices.C: It masks sensitive columns in query results
choices.D: QUERY_HISTORY performs data loading operations and ETL transformations

questions.explanation: QUERY_HISTORY surfaces patterns—slow queries, repeated expensive queries, large scans—so you can prioritize optimizations (clustering, rewriting, caching, right-sizing) with the most impact.

questions.question: Which is the most accurate statement about materialized views vs. result cache for repeated heavy aggregations?

choices.A: Materialized views and the result cache are functionally interchangeable, since both simply store finished query output and replay it for every subsequent repeated request
choices.B: Result cache only helps identical queries on unchanged data; materialized views persist results and help across varying queries on slowly-changing data
choices.C: The result cache persists computed results indefinitely and keeps serving them even after the base tables change, so a materialized view would add no value
choices.D: Materialized views need no ongoing maintenance once created, because Snowflake freezes their stored output and never has to refresh it when underlying data changes

questions.explanation: The result cache helps only when the exact query repeats on unchanged data. Materialized views maintain stored aggregates that can accelerate a range of queries even as they differ, at the cost of storage and maintenance.

questions.question: Which is the recommended way to prevent a department from exceeding its monthly credit budget?

choices.A: Monitor departmental usage manually on a daily basis
choices.B: Assign a resource monitor with a quota and suspend action to the department's warehouse(s)
choices.C: Permanently disable the warehouse to prevent overspend
choices.D: Increase the warehouse size to accommodate unlimited queries

questions.explanation: A resource monitor with a credit quota and suspend thresholds on the department's warehouses automatically enforces the budget, sending alerts and suspending compute when limits are reached.

questions.question: Which is true about resource monitor schedules/frequency?

choices.A: Credit quotas can only be configured on a monthly basis
choices.B: Quotas can reset on a schedule (e.g., daily, weekly, monthly, yearly) or never
choices.C: Resource monitor quotas cannot be reset
choices.D: Quotas reset every minute automatically

questions.explanation: Resource monitor credit quotas can be configured to reset on various frequencies (daily, weekly, monthly, yearly) or set to never reset, aligning with budgeting cycles.

questions.question: Which is a reason that selecting fewer columns improves performance in Snowflake?

choices.A: Row-oriented storage reads all columns in every scan
choices.B: Columnar storage means fewer columns scanned equals less I/O
choices.C: Column selection modifies the warehouse size
choices.D: Selecting fewer columns disables partition pruning

questions.explanation: Snowflake's columnar format reads only requested columns. Selecting fewer columns reduces the data scanned and thus improves performance and lowers cost.

questions.question: Which is the best explanation for why an overly large warehouse can waste money on small queries?

choices.A: Small queries can't use the extra parallelism, so you pay a higher credit rate for no speedup
choices.B: Large warehouse configurations are consistently slower
choices.C: Large warehouses increase storage costs, which affects total quarterly bills
choices.D: Oversized warehouses disable caching mechanisms and query optimization

questions.explanation: Small or non-parallelizable queries can't exploit a large warehouse's extra compute, so you pay the higher per-second credit rate without proportional performance gains—wasting money.

questions.question: Which is a good practice for warehouses used by scheduled ETL jobs?

choices.A: Keep ETL warehouses running continuously 24/7
choices.B: Use auto-suspend/auto-resume and right-size so they run only during jobs
choices.C: Use the largest warehouse size for all ETL operations
choices.D: Disable monitoring and tracking of ETL warehouse

questions.explanation: ETL warehouses should auto-resume for jobs and auto-suspend afterward, sized appropriately for the workload, so credits are spent only during actual processing.

questions.question: Which approach helps a query that is slow primarily due to a single non-selective predicate scanning most of a huge table?

choices.A: Such non-selective queries have reached a hard limit and cannot be improved further by any data model, clustering, or warehouse change
choices.B: Reconsider the data model/clustering or pre-aggregate via a materialized view, since pruning can't help a non-selective scan
choices.C: Lower the AUTO_SUSPEND setting so the warehouse idles sooner, which shortens the non-selective scan and speeds up the query
choices.D: Disable the result cache for the non-selective query, forcing fresh computation that the optimizer can then prune more aggressively

questions.explanation: If the predicate is inherently non-selective (matches most rows), pruning offers little benefit. Solutions include remodeling, pre-aggregation (materialized views), or scaling up compute to process the large scan faster.

questions.question: Which is true about the COPY INTO load metadata used to avoid duplicates?

choices.A: Load metadata is retained indefinitely for all tables
choices.B: It is retained for 64 days per table for bulk COPY duplicate detection
choices.C: Load metadata is kept for only 24 hours per table
choices.D: Load metadata is never stored or tracked by Snowflake

questions.explanation: Bulk COPY load history (for skipping already-loaded files) is retained for 64 days per table. After that window, previously loaded files could be reloaded if presented again.

questions.question: Which is the best practice for ongoing ingestion of frequently arriving files with minimal management?

choices.A: Execute manual COPY each time files arrive for ingestion processing
choices.B: Snowpipe auto-ingest, optionally combined with streams and tasks for downstream processing
choices.C: Reload the entire dataset daily with FORCE = TRUE
choices.D: Use external tables exclusively without additional loading or staging

questions.explanation: Snowpipe auto-ingest provides hands-off continuous loading; pairing it with streams and tasks enables automated incremental transformation downstream—an efficient modern ingestion pipeline.

questions.question: Which file format type would you choose to preserve nested structure and types efficiently for loading?

choices.A: CSV
choices.B: Parquet (or Avro/ORC)
choices.C: Fixed-width text
choices.D: Plain text logs

questions.explanation: Columnar/typed formats like Parquet (and Avro/ORC) preserve schema, types, and nested structures and are compressed, making them efficient and reliable for loading complex data.

questions.question: Which is the best way to load a continuous stream of rows from an application with the lowest latency and no files?

choices.A: Bulk COPY hourly
choices.B: Snowpipe Streaming (rowset API)
choices.C: INSERT one row at a time over JDBC in a tight loop
choices.D: External tables

questions.explanation: Snowpipe Streaming ingests rows directly via an API with low latency and without staging files, making it ideal for continuous application/event streams compared to file-based or single-row INSERT approaches.

questions.question: Which is true about unloading data and the number of output files?

choices.A: Unload operations always emit exactly one output file per execution, regardless of data volume or parallelism settings
choices.B: By default multiple files may be produced (based on parallelism/MAX_FILE_SIZE); SINGLE = TRUE forces one
choices.C: Unloaded output is always written in raw, uncompressed form, so the file count cannot be influenced by compression
choices.D: The number of files produced by an unload cannot be influenced by any parameter, since Snowflake decides it internally

questions.explanation: Unloading typically yields multiple files for parallelism, governed by MAX_FILE_SIZE and threads. SINGLE = TRUE forces a single file, and PARTITION BY can organize output into directories.

questions.question: Which is correct about loading data with embedded newlines in quoted CSV fields?

choices.A: Embedded newlines in quoted CSV fields cannot be loaded successfully
choices.B: Set FIELD_OPTIONALLY_ENCLOSED_BY so quoted fields containing newlines are parsed correctly
choices.C: All quotes must be removed from CSV before loading
choices.D: JSON format should be used exclusively for embedded newlines in fields

questions.explanation: Configuring FIELD_OPTIONALLY_ENCLOSED_BY (e.g., a double quote) lets the parser treat newlines inside quoted fields as data rather than record delimiters, correctly handling multiline values.

questions.question: Which is true about the relationship between Snowpipe and a virtual warehouse?

choices.A: Snowpipe requires you to allocate and run a warehouse
choices.B: Snowpipe uses Snowflake-managed serverless compute, not your virtual warehouse
choices.C: Snowpipe runs on the client machine as a local service
choices.D: Snowpipe requires a minimum 6XL warehouse capacity

questions.explanation: Snowpipe is serverless; it uses Snowflake-managed compute and bills separately, so you don't provision or run a virtual warehouse for it.

questions.question: Which is the best way to handle loading where the same file may be delivered twice by an upstream system?

choices.A: Always set FORCE = TRUE so the files reload every time, then detect and remove the duplicate rows after loading
choices.B: Rely on default load-history dedup (skip already-loaded files) so re-delivered files aren't loaded again
choices.C: Maintain an external spreadsheet that manually tracks which files were delivered, checking it before each load
choices.D: Disable load-metadata tracking so the same file reloads on every delivery, accepting the duplicated rows downstream

questions.explanation: Snowflake's default load history detects already-loaded files (by name/ETag) and skips them, preventing duplicates from re-delivered files—provided you don't override with FORCE = TRUE.

questions.question: Which is true about the GET command and external stages?

choices.A: GET downloads files directly from external cloud storage
choices.B: GET works only with internal stages; use cloud tools for external storage
choices.C: GET can upload files to internal and external stages
choices.D: GET loads data directly into tables without COPY

questions.explanation: GET downloads from internal stages only. For external storage, you retrieve files using the cloud provider's tooling, not GET.

questions.question: Which is the best approach to validate a complex CSV's structure before a production load?

choices.A: Load the CSV into production and rely on downstream systems
choices.B: Run COPY with VALIDATION_MODE to surface errors without loading, then fix issues
choices.C: Use FORCE = TRUE to load and log errors without blocking
choices.D: Delete the target table and start fresh to validate

questions.explanation: VALIDATION_MODE (e.g., RETURN_ERRORS / RETURN_ALL_ERRORS) lets you validate the file against the load definition and inspect errors before committing to a production load.

questions.question: Which is correct about the FLATTEN function's OUTER parameter?

choices.A: OUTER => TRUE removes rows with empty arrays or null input data
choices.B: OUTER => TRUE keeps a row (with NULLs) even when the input array/object is empty or null
choices.C: OUTER has no effect on the result set
choices.D: OUTER pivots row values into separate columns for dimensional analysis

questions.explanation: With OUTER => TRUE, FLATTEN emits a row even when the input is empty or NULL (similar to an outer join), preventing the parent row from being dropped when there are no elements to expand.

questions.question: Which is true about combining streams with MERGE in a task for CDC processing?

choices.A: MERGE and streams are incompatible for CDC and incremental processing
choices.B: A task can MERGE a stream's changes into a target, and consuming the stream advances its offset
choices.C: Streams cannot feed source data into MERGE
choices.D: MERGE ignores METADATA$ACTION and METADATA$ISUPDATE in stream processing

questions.explanation: A common pattern: a scheduled task runs a MERGE using a stream as the source of changes (with METADATA$ACTION/ISUPDATE), applying inserts/updates/deletes to a target. Consuming the stream in the committed MERGE advances its offset.

questions.question: Which metadata column on a standard stream indicates the type of change (INSERT/DELETE)?

choices.A: METADATA$ACTION
choices.B: METADATA$FILENAME
choices.C: METADATA$ROW_ID only
choices.D: METADATA$TYPE

questions.explanation: A stream exposes METADATA$ACTION (INSERT or DELETE) and METADATA$ISUPDATE (whether the change is part of an update) so consumers can apply the appropriate operation to a target.

questions.question: How does a standard stream represent an UPDATE to a row?

choices.A: A standard stream records it as a single UPDATE row that carries both the old and the new column values together
choices.B: As a DELETE of the old row and an INSERT of the new row, both flagged with METADATA$ISUPDATE = TRUE
choices.C: A standard stream records it as only an INSERT carrying the new column values, discarding the prior row entirely
choices.D: Updates are ignored entirely by a standard stream, which tracks only inserted and deleted rows but no modifications

questions.explanation: A standard stream models an update as a paired DELETE (old version) and INSERT (new version), with METADATA$ISUPDATE = TRUE on both, allowing consumers to reconstruct the change.

questions.question: Which is the correct way to reference an element of an ARRAY column a at index 0?

choices.C: a->0 only
choices.D: ELEMENT(a, 0)

questions.explanation: Array elements are accessed with bracket notation using a zero-based index, e.g., a[0]. The result is a VARIANT that may need casting to a specific type.

questions.question: Which is true about deterministic results from ROW_NUMBER() when ordering ties exist?

choices.A: Ties always receive the same number without variation between runs
choices.B: Without a fully deterministic ORDER BY, tie ordering may be arbitrary between runs
choices.C: ROW_NUMBER ignores the ORDER BY clause
choices.D: ROW_NUMBER throws an error when encountering ties in ordering

questions.explanation: ROW_NUMBER() assigns distinct numbers, but if the ORDER BY does not uniquely order tied rows, which tied row gets which number can vary. Add tie-breaker columns for fully deterministic results.

questions.question: Which is the function to convert a value to a string representation?

choices.A: TO_VARCHAR / CAST(... AS VARCHAR)
choices.B: PARSE_JSON
choices.C: FLATTEN
choices.D: TO_VARIANT

questions.explanation: TO_VARCHAR (or ::VARCHAR / CAST AS VARCHAR) converts values to string form, optionally with a format for dates/numbers.

questions.question: Which is correct about the performance impact of secure UDFs in shared logic?

choices.A: Secure UDFs always execute faster than regular UDFs, because protecting their logic lets the optimizer take extra shortcuts
choices.B: Like secure views, they may forgo some optimizations to protect logic/data, potentially impacting performance
choices.C: Secure UDFs cannot be shared across accounts, so they provide no advantage when distributing logic to data share consumers
choices.D: Secure UDFs expose their full source code to consumers, trading confidentiality for the speed of normal inlined functions

questions.explanation: Secure UDFs hide their definition and avoid optimizations that might leak data, similar to secure views. This protection can come with some performance cost compared to non-secure equivalents.

questions.question: Which is true about the COALESCE vs. IFNULL functions?

choices.A: IFNULL accepts an unlimited list of arguments exactly like COALESCE, returning the first non-NULL value among them
choices.B: COALESCE accepts multiple arguments and returns the first non-NULL; IFNULL handles two arguments
choices.C: Neither COALESCE nor IFNULL is able to evaluate or handle NULL values, so both raise an error on NULL input
choices.D: COALESCE works only with numeric data types and other directly comparable values, rejecting strings and dates

questions.explanation: COALESCE evaluates a list and returns the first non-NULL value; IFNULL(a, b) is the two-argument case returning b when a is NULL.

questions.question: Which best describes how to incrementally process only new rows from a source table daily?

choices.A: Re-scan the entire source table every day and compare it against yesterday's full copy to find the rows that are new
choices.B: Create a stream on the table and a task that processes the stream's changes, advancing the offset each run
choices.C: Rely on the result cache to skip already-processed rows, since it automatically remembers which records were read before
choices.D: Clone the source table each day and diff the clones, using the storage snapshots to identify the newly inserted rows

questions.explanation: A stream tracks new/changed rows; a scheduled task consumes those changes (e.g., via INSERT/MERGE), advancing the offset so each run processes only the delta—efficient incremental processing.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which best describes the benefit of Snowflake's automatic micro-partition metadata for COUNT queries?

choices.A: Simple COUNT queries require a full table scan without exceptions
choices.B: Simple counts and min/max can be answered from metadata without scanning data or a warehouse
choices.C: COUNT queries are not supported in Snowflake SQL
choices.D: COUNT query results always use the result cache mechanism exclusively

questions.explanation: Because Snowflake keeps per-partition statistics, queries like SELECT COUNT(*) or MIN/MAX of a column can often be served from metadata in Cloud Services, avoiding data scans and warehouse usage.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which best explains why two warehouses can have very different performance on the same query?

choices.A: Storage content differs between warehouses in the same account
choices.B: Different sizes (compute/memory) and cache warmth lead to different performance
choices.C: Each warehouse maintains and owns its own independent data copy
choices.D: Query performance variations are completely random and unpredictable

questions.explanation: Warehouses differ in size (compute/memory) and in how warm their local cache is. A larger or warmer warehouse can run the same query faster; the underlying shared data is identical.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is true about Snowflake's relationship to the underlying cloud object storage?

choices.A: Customers directly manage and manipulate the raw files in cloud object storage, organizing the partitions and folders themselves
choices.B: Snowflake manages storage internally as optimized micro-partitions; customers interact via SQL, not raw files
choices.C: Data is physically stored on local SSDs attached to the compute warehouse nodes rather than in cloud object storage
choices.D: There is no underlying storage layer at all in Snowflake; tables exist only transiently in warehouse memory during queries

questions.explanation: For internal (native) tables, Snowflake stores data as optimized, compressed micro-partitions in cloud object storage that it manages; users interact through SQL rather than handling raw files.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is the most accurate statement about Snowflake's scalability for storage?

choices.A: Storage capacity is permanently capped at a fixed maximum size
choices.B: Storage scales virtually without limit on cloud object storage, independent of compute
choices.C: Storage can only scale when new compute warehouse resources are added
choices.D: Storage requires manual provisioning of infrastructure

questions.explanation: Snowflake leverages near-limitless cloud object storage that scales independently of compute, so you can grow data volume without provisioning hardware or coupling it to warehouse sizing.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is a correct statement about Snowflake editions and feature availability?

choices.A: All Snowflake editions provide exactly the same feature set, so the chosen edition affects only the negotiated pricing
choices.B: Higher editions add features (e.g., multi-cluster at Enterprise, advanced security at Business Critical)
choices.C: The Standard edition includes the most comprehensive feature set, and higher editions progressively remove capabilities
choices.D: Virtual Private Snowflake offers the fewest features of any edition despite being positioned as the most isolated tier

questions.explanation: Features increase with edition: Enterprise adds multi-cluster, extended Time Travel, materialized views, etc.; Business Critical adds advanced security/compliance; VPS adds isolation—each building on the prior.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is true about how Time Travel and cloning rely on the same underlying mechanism?

choices.A: Time Travel and cloning are completely unrelated mechanisms built on separate subsystems that share no underlying storage behavior
choices.B: Both leverage immutable micro-partitions and metadata pointers, enabling historical access and zero-copy clones
choices.C: Both Time Travel and cloning physically copy and duplicate the table's data in storage at the moment that the operation is run
choices.D: Both Time Travel and cloning depend on configuring a Fail-safe retention period before either feature can be used at all

questions.explanation: Immutable micro-partitions plus metadata versioning underpin both Time Travel (referencing prior partition sets) and zero-copy cloning (pointing to existing partitions), making both efficient.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is true about the PUBLIC schema in a new database?

choices.A: The PUBLIC schema is never created automatically and must be added manually before any objects can be stored in it
choices.B: It is created automatically and can hold objects unless you organize them into custom schemas
choices.C: The PUBLIC schema is read-only and cannot be modified, so objects must always be placed into custom schemas instead
choices.D: The PUBLIC schema exclusively stores system metadata objects and is not available for user-created tables or views

questions.explanation: Every new database includes a default PUBLIC schema (and INFORMATION_SCHEMA). You can use PUBLIC or create custom schemas to organize objects.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which best describes how Snowflake supports both data warehouse and data lake patterns?

choices.A: Only traditional structured data-warehouse patterns are supported, so raw files in object storage cannot be queried in place
choices.B: Native tables for warehousing plus external/Iceberg tables and stages for data lake access over object storage
choices.C: Only unstructured file storage is offered, without any SQL query capability over data-lake content or warehouse-style tables
choices.D: Neither data-warehouse nor data-lake access patterns are supported natively, so external engines are required for both

questions.explanation: Snowflake supports warehousing with native tables and data lake patterns via external tables, Iceberg tables, and stages over cloud object storage—unifying both on one platform.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is true about how queries are billed when served entirely from the result cache?

choices.A: They consume warehouse compute credits as normal
choices.B: They consume no warehouse compute credits because no warehouse runs
choices.C: They incur double the cost of regular query execution
choices.D: They are billed exclusively as storage consumption charges

questions.explanation: A result-cache hit returns data from Cloud Services without starting a warehouse, so no compute credits are consumed for that query.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which is the most accurate description of Snowflake's 'single platform' value proposition?

choices.A: It specializes narrowly in extract, transform, and load pipelines and leaves storage, sharing, and analytics to separate external tools
choices.B: It consolidates storage, multi-workload compute, governance, sharing, apps, and AI in one governed platform across clouds
choices.C: It is primarily a client-side desktop application that performs local computing on the user's own machine rather than in the cloud
choices.D: It replaces all SQL-based access with a graphical user interface, so workloads must be built without writing any queries at all

questions.explanation: Snowflake's value is consolidating diverse workloads—warehouse, lake, engineering, sharing, apps, AI—under unified storage, security, and governance across multiple clouds, reducing silos and integration overhead.

questions.question: Which is true about who can create a reader account?

choices.A: Any data-share consumer can create reader accounts for themselves once they have accepted an inbound share
choices.B: The data provider (with ACCOUNTADMIN or appropriate privileges) creates and manages reader accounts
choices.C: Only Snowflake support staff are able to create and manage reader accounts on behalf of a data provider
choices.D: Reader accounts are created automatically the moment a consumer accepts a share, with no provider action needed

questions.explanation: Reader accounts are created and managed by the data provider (typically via ACCOUNTADMIN). The provider controls users and pays for the reader account's compute.

questions.question: Which is the best way to share data that must remain governed by masking even for the consumer?

choices.A: Share a non-secure view without any masking or row-level filtering
choices.B: Share secure views over tables with masking/row policies so governance travels with the data
choices.C: Export to CSV files and send encrypted attachments to the consumer
choices.D: Grant ACCOUNTADMIN privilege to the consumer for complete access

questions.explanation: By sharing secure views with masking and row access policies, the provider ensures consumers only see governed, masked/filtered data—governance is enforced at query time regardless of consumer.

questions.question: Which is true about Time Travel querying syntax with a query ID?

choices.A: BEFORE(STATEMENT => '<query_id>') returns data as it was just before that statement executed
choices.B: Query IDs cannot be used in Time Travel queries for historical recovery
choices.C: Only timestamps are allowed in Time Travel queries; statement IDs are not supported
choices.D: It returns future data that will exist after the specified statement executes

questions.explanation: Using BEFORE(STATEMENT => '<query_id>') retrieves the table state immediately prior to the specified statement, which is handy for undoing the effect of a known problematic query.

questions.question: Which is the correct consideration when relying on Fail-safe for recovery?

choices.A: It is instant and self-service with immediate recovery guarantees
choices.B: It is a last resort requiring Snowflake support, not guaranteed-time self-service recovery
choices.C: It replaces traditional backups entirely with guaranteed recovery SLAs
choices.D: It is configurable per table with varying recovery time windows

questions.explanation: Fail-safe is a best-effort, Snowflake-assisted recovery mechanism (not self-service) for the 7 days after Time Travel. It should not be treated as a primary backup/recovery strategy.

questions.question: Which is true about cloning a database for a dev environment?

choices.A: It duplicates all storage immediately, consuming the full data size upfront
choices.B: It clones contained schemas/tables via zero-copy, providing an independent dev copy efficiently
choices.C: It shares write access with production; changes affect both dev and prod
choices.D: It is not allowed to clone a database for development or testing

questions.explanation: Cloning a database performs zero-copy clones of its schemas and tables, giving developers an independent, low-cost copy of production data to work with without duplicating storage upfront.

questions.question: Which is the best way to ensure a partner always sees current data without you running exports?

choices.A: Nightly CSV exports that are transferred to the partner's infrastructure
choices.B: Secure Data Sharing, which gives live read-only access to current data
choices.C: Emailing spreadsheets with the latest data extracts periodically
choices.D: Granting them a copy of your warehouse for independent query execution

questions.explanation: Secure Data Sharing provides live, always-current, read-only access to the shared objects—eliminating exports and ensuring partners query the latest data directly.

questions.question: Which is true about how masking interacts with stored data?

choices.A: Masking encrypts stored data using additional encryption layers
choices.B: Masking transforms values only at query time; stored data is unchanged
choices.C: Masking deletes and purges masked data from storage permanently
choices.D: Masking changes the column data type and structure permanently

questions.explanation: Dynamic data masking alters how values appear in query results based on policy/role, but the underlying stored data remains intact and unmodified.

questions.question: Which combination best supports a compliance requirement to audit who accessed PII columns and to mask them for non-privileged roles?

choices.A: Result cache configuration combined with sequence generators
choices.B: ACCESS_HISTORY for column-level audit plus masking policies (ideally tag-based) on PII columns
choices.C: Network policies that restrict access by IP address and geography
choices.D: Fail-safe recovery that maintains archived copies of sensitive data

questions.explanation: ACCESS_HISTORY records column-level access for auditing, while masking policies (often tag-based via data classification) ensure PII is masked for non-privileged roles—together meeting audit and protection requirements.

questions.question: Which is true about the read-only nature of shared databases for consumers?

choices.A: Consumers can create tables inside the shared database for their own use
choices.B: Consumers cannot create or modify objects in the shared database; it is read-only
choices.C: Consumers can drop the provider's tables to remove unwanted shared objects
choices.D: Consumers own the shared data and can modify it as needed

questions.explanation: A database created from an inbound share is strictly read-only; consumers cannot add or change objects within it. They can, however, query it and combine results with their own writable databases.

questions.question: Which is the most complete description of Snowflake's layered data protection for a permanent table?

choices.A: Only encryption at rest is provided; encryption in transit is not included
choices.B: Automatic AES-256 encryption, configurable Time Travel (up to 90 days), and a fixed 7-day Fail-safe
choices.C: Only Time Travel is provided; encryption and Fail-safe are separate features
choices.D: Only Fail-safe is provided; Time Travel and encryption are optional

questions.explanation: A permanent table is protected by always-on AES-256 encryption (in transit and at rest), configurable Time Travel (up to 90 days on Enterprise+), and a non-configurable 7-day Fail-safe—providing layered protection and recoverability.

questions.question: Which statement correctly removes the stored procedure SALES_REPORT(MONTH INT, YEAR INT)?

choices.A: DROP PROCEDURE SALES_REPORT(INT, INT);
choices.B: DROP PROCEDURE SALES_REPORT;
choices.C: DROP PROCEDURE SALES_REPORT(MONTH, YEAR);
choices.D: DELETE PROCEDURE SALES_REPORT(INT, INT);

questions.explanation: Dropping a procedure requires the name plus the argument data types (not the parameter names): DROP PROCEDURE SALES_REPORT(INT, INT). The signature disambiguates overloaded procedures.

questions.question: Which sampling form returns a fixed number of rows rather than a percentage of the table?

choices.A: SAMPLE / TABLESAMPLE using the ROWS form, e.g., SAMPLE (10 ROWS)
choices.B: SAMPLE with percentage (e.g., SAMPLE (10))
choices.C: BERNOULLI sampling with exact row counts
choices.D: SYSTEM/BLOCK sampling returns fixed row numbers

questions.explanation: Fixed-size sampling uses the row-count form SAMPLE (n ROWS), returning exactly n rows. The percentage forms (BERNOULLI/SYSTEM) return an approximate fraction of rows.

questions.question: Which Snowflake mechanism lets you override a table's natural clustering to improve pruning?

choices.A: Clustering keys
choices.B: Materialized views
choices.C: Result cache
choices.D: Search Optimization Service

questions.explanation: Defining a clustering key reorganizes data by chosen columns, overriding the natural (load-order) clustering and improving pruning for queries filtering on those columns.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: What is the effect on existing micro-partitions when a column is dropped from a table?

choices.A: Existing micro-partitions are not rewritten; the column is removed via metadata
choices.B: All existing micro-partitions are immediately rewritten
choices.C: The entire table is recreated with a new structure
choices.D: Adjacent micro-partitions are automatically merged together

questions.explanation: Dropping a column is a metadata-only operation. Existing micro-partitions are left unchanged and the column is simply no longer exposed.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: When does Snowflake perform micro-partitioning of table data?

choices.A: Automatically as data is loaded into the table
choices.B: Only when a clustering key is defined
choices.C: When the user runs a manual PARTITION command
choices.D: During the Fail-safe period

questions.explanation: Snowflake automatically divides data into micro-partitions as it is loaded; there is no manual partitioning step.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: What happens when a single row in a table is updated?

choices.A: The micro-partition containing the row is replaced by a new one
choices.B: Only the single row's bytes are edited in place
choices.C: The entire table is rewritten
choices.D: Nothing changes on disk until Fail-safe

questions.explanation: Micro-partitions are immutable, so an update writes a new micro-partition with the changed data and supersedes the old one, which also enables Time Travel.

questions.question: Which table type has no Fail-safe period?

choices.A: Transient table
choices.B: Permanent table
choices.C: Standard table
choices.D: Managed table

questions.explanation: Transient (and temporary) tables have no Fail-safe, lowering storage cost. Permanent tables include a 7-day Fail-safe.

questions.question: Which table type is visible only within the session that created it?

choices.A: Temporary table
choices.B: Transient table
choices.C: Permanent table
choices.D: External table

questions.explanation: A temporary table exists only for its creating session and is automatically dropped when that session ends.

questions.domain: Snowflake AI Data Cloud Features and Architecture
questions.question: Which Snowflake table type is optimized for transactional (OLTP-style) workloads?

choices.A: Hybrid table (Unistore)
choices.B: Transient table
choices.C: External table
choices.D: Temporary table

questions.explanation: Hybrid tables (Unistore) add primary keys and row-level indexes for fast single-row reads/writes, supporting transactional workloads alongside analytics.

questions.question: What is the maximum data recovery window for a transient table using Time Travel?

choices.A: 0 to 1 day
choices.B: Up to 7 days
choices.C: Up to 90 days
choices.D: 0 days only

questions.explanation: Transient tables support Time Travel of 0 or 1 day and have no Fail-safe, so the maximum recovery window is 1 day.

questions.question: What is the default Time Travel retention period for a table?

choices.A: 1 day
choices.B: 0 days
choices.C: 7 days
choices.D: 90 days

questions.explanation: The default DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS is 1 day for all editions; Enterprise+ can extend permanent objects up to 90 days.

questions.question: What is the key characteristic of Time Travel?

choices.A: It lets you access or restore historical data within the retention period
choices.B: It permanently archives data forever without any expiration or purge
choices.C: It replicates data across regions and cloud platforms automatically
choices.D: It encrypts data at rest using asymmetric encryption algorithms

questions.explanation: Time Travel enables querying, cloning, and restoring data as it existed at points within the configured retention window.

questions.question: When the account MIN_DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS and an object's DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS differ, which value applies?

choices.A: The greater of the two values
choices.B: The lesser of the two values
choices.C: Always the account-level value
choices.D: Always the object-level value

questions.explanation: MIN_DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS sets a floor; the effective retention is the greater of the account minimum and the object's own setting.

questions.question: Which of the following consume storage that Snowflake bills?

choices.A: All of these: permanent tables, materialized views, transient tables, and Fail-safe
choices.B: Only permanent tables consume billable storage; other object types are free
choices.C: Only materialized views consume storage; tables and Fail-safe data do not
choices.D: Only Fail-safe data consumes storage; active data is stored separately

questions.explanation: Permanent/transient tables, materialized views, and Time Travel/Fail-safe data all occupy billable storage.

questions.question: Which warehouse setting enables horizontal scaling (scale-out) for concurrency?

choices.A: MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT
choices.B: WAREHOUSE_SIZE
choices.C: AUTO_SUSPEND
choices.D: STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS

questions.explanation: Setting MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT greater than 1 creates a multi-cluster warehouse that scales out additional clusters to absorb concurrency.

questions.question: Which feature temporarily adds serverless compute to accelerate occasional heavy queries without resizing the warehouse?

choices.A: Query Acceleration Service
choices.B: Search Optimization Service
choices.C: Result cache
choices.D: Automatic Clustering

questions.explanation: QAS offloads portions of eligible large scan/filter queries onto shared serverless compute, accelerating outliers without permanently scaling the warehouse.

questions.question: Which privilege allows a role to start, stop, and resume a virtual warehouse?

choices.A: OPERATE
choices.B: USAGE
choices.C: MONITOR
choices.D: MODIFY

questions.explanation: OPERATE permits starting/stopping/suspending/resuming a warehouse; USAGE only allows running queries on it.

questions.question: Which multi-cluster scaling policy minimizes credits by being conservative about starting clusters?

choices.A: ECONOMY
choices.B: STANDARD
choices.C: MAXIMIZED
choices.D: AGGRESSIVE

questions.explanation: The ECONOMY scaling policy favors credit conservation, starting another cluster only when there is enough load to keep it busy for about 6 minutes.

questions.question: What is the recommended way to optimize a mixed workload of continuous loading and reporting?

choices.A: Use separate, dedicated warehouses for each workload
choices.B: Run both on one extra-small warehouse
choices.C: Disable auto-suspend
choices.D: Rely solely on the result cache

questions.explanation: Isolating loading and reporting on separate warehouses prevents resource contention and allows independent sizing, scaling, and cost attribution.

questions.question: Which SQL command does Snowpipe use under the hood to load data?

choices.A: COPY INTO
choices.B: INSERT
choices.C: PUT
choices.D: MERGE

questions.explanation: A pipe wraps a COPY INTO <table> statement that Snowpipe runs automatically as new files arrive.

questions.question: Which COPY INTO ON_ERROR option prevents a partial load by failing the whole statement on any error?

choices.A: ON_ERROR = ABORT_STATEMENT
choices.B: ON_ERROR = CONTINUE
choices.C: ON_ERROR = SKIP_FILE
choices.D: ON_ERROR = SKIP_FILE_10

questions.explanation: ABORT_STATEMENT stops and rolls back the load on the first error, avoiding partially loaded data. It is the default for bulk COPY.

questions.question: Which COPY option validates files and returns errors without loading any data?

choices.A: VALIDATION_MODE = RETURN_ERRORS
choices.B: ON_ERROR = CONTINUE
choices.C: FORCE = TRUE
choices.D: PURGE = TRUE

questions.explanation: VALIDATION_MODE (e.g., RETURN_ERRORS / RETURN_ALL_ERRORS) checks staged files and reports issues without inserting any rows.
